title: SFAT Application No. 2/2023
text:
Application No. 2 of 2023
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by
the Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures
Ordinance, Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of
the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
RAFFAELLO CAPITAL LIMITED Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr. Michael Hartmann, GBS, Chairman
Mr. Webster Ng Kam-wah, JP, Member
Ms. Ivy Chua Suk-lin, Member
Date of Hearing: 20 – 23 January 2025
Date of Filing Final Written Submissions: 24 March 2025
Determination: 22 September 2025
____________________
DETERMINATION
________________________

A A
Introduction
B B
1. At all times material to this application for review, the
C Applicant, RaffAello Capital Limited (‘RaffAello’), was licensed by the C
Respondent, the Securities and Futures Commission (the ‘SFC’), to carry on
D D
Type 6 regulated activities pursuant to the provisions of the Securities and
E Futures Ordinance (the ‘Ordinance’). Type 6 regulated activities encompass E
the giving of advice on matters of corporate finance and, as in the present case,
F F
acting as ‘sponsors’ to corporations seeking to be listed on the Hong Kong
G
Stock Market.
G
H H
2. Sponsors, in acting as advisers to corporations seeking listing,
I play a pivotal role in bringing those corporations to the Hong Kong market. I
They do so by undertaking a fundamental obligation to ensure that all required
J J
information about corporations seeking listing is accurate, complete and not,
K
directly or indirectly, misleading. The means by which a sponsor discharges
K
this obligation is, of course, an investigative one, that is, by way of the
L L
exercise of due diligence.
M M
3. In this regard, General Principle 2 of the Code of Conduct for
N N
Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and Futures
O
Commission (‘the Code of Conduct’) directs that sponsors must conduct due
O
diligence on an applicant for listing and, in doing so, must exercise due skill,
P P
care and diligence in the best interests of clients and the integrity of the
Q Q
market.
R R
4. More will be said of the dynamics of ‘due diligence’ later as it
S S
is a concept central to the determination of this matter.
T T
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
A brief background
B B
5. On 14 February 2017, the Applicant, RaffAello, was appointed
C to act as sole sponsor to a company seeking listing on the Growth Enterprise C
Market (‘GEM’) of the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.
D D
E 6. At that time, Tsang Kin Hung (‘Ricky Tsang’) was a director of E
RaffAello and one of its responsible officers. He was to acquire full ownership
F F
of the company in 2018.
G G
7. The company seeking listing on the GEM was Paprika Holdings
H H
Limited (‘Paprika’ or ‘the Paprika Group’). The Hong Kong GEM market is
I not intended for large, well-established businesses. The GEM market serves I
the needs of small and mid-size issuers looking to raise capital to fund their
J J
growth. It follows therefore that, in respect of companies seeking listing on
K the GEM, potential investors look for growth potential. K
L L
8. Paprika, as a holding company, acting together with its
M subsidiaries, carried on business as sellers of handbags and related M
accessories, doing so under the brands of ‘Paprika’ and ‘Paprika Edition’. The
N N
handbags and accessories sold by Paprika were not ‘high-end’ luxury items.
O The evidence shows that the merchandise was aimed rather at the mass O
market: relatively cheaper items therefore but sold in greater numbers.
P P
Q 9. The founder, CEO and Chairman of Paprika was Leung Shi Q
Wai, Samuel (‘Samuel Leung’).
R R
S 10. In order to put Paprika’s listing application together and present S
it to the Stock Exchange, RaffAello, in its role of sponsor, assembled a
T T
transaction team. The transaction team consisted of eight persons: five ‘core
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
members’ and three ‘associates’1. Ricky Tsang, RaffAello’s sole director, was
B B
not a member of the transaction team.
C C
11. The transaction team’s responsibilities included the preparation
D of two documents that are fundamental to all applications for listing on the D
GEM; first, a draft prospectus and, second, evidence of Paprika’s ‘track
E E
record’ over the previous two years.
F F
12. A ‘track record’ refers to the past performance and history of a
G G
company seeking listing, particularly its financial performance and
H management record. This includes details of earnings, debt levels and, H
importantly in this matter, growth history. In respect of the Paprika application
I I
for listing, the two-year track record period covered the two years ending
J
31 March 2016 and 31 March 2017.
J
K K
13. In order to render professional assistance in the listing process,
L RSM Hong Kong (‘RSM’) was appointed to act as the reporting accountant L
while Reed Smith Richards Butler (‘RSRB’) was appointed to act as legal
M M
advisor.
N N
14. The Paprika Group did not itself manufacture handbags and
O O
accessories. These were manufactured and/or obtained for it by various
P suppliers. One of the suppliers, which at all times was held out as being an P
independent business, was API Trading Company Limited.
Q Q
R 15. The Paprika Group marketed and sold its handbags and R
accessories to both retail customers and to wholesalers which then ‘on-sold’
S S
the merchandise. During the track record period the retail network of the
T T
1
Under the relevant Code of Conduct, a sponsor is obliged to ensure that it has sufficient staff to satisfy its
U obligations and responsibilities. U
- 4 -
V V

A A
Paprika Group covered 15 retail stores and two concessionaire counters in
B B
Hong Kong.
C C
16. The Paprika track record showed that retail sales were the
D dominant driver, constituting something like 90% of revenue. As for the D
wholesale side of the business, constituting the remaining 10% of revenue, the
E E
Paprika Group made sales to various wholesale businesses which would then
F F
on-sell the products to their own retail customers in Hong Kong and also to
Mainland customers.
G G
H 17. Paprika’s Application for Listing was submitted on 14 June H
2017, some two and a half months after the closure of the two-year track
I I
record period. A number of amended applications were submitted at a later
J
time but it is important to recognise that it is this first application which is the
J
centre of focus in this application for review.
K K
L 18. The SFC has focused on the fact that, at the time of its original L
submission, Paprika’s Application for Listing was required to be effectively
M M
complete; in short, it was a regulatory requirement that all necessary due
N
diligence must have been conducted and completed up to that date. In this
N
regard, paragraph 17(2)(b) of the Code of Conduct directs that:
O O
P “… before submitting a listing application a sponsor should complete all P
reasonable due diligence on a listing applicant except in relation to matters
that by their nature can only be dealt with at a later date.”
Q Q
R 19. According to the prospectus filed as part of the listing R
application on 14 June 2017, over the two-year track record period the Paprika
S S
Group had experienced a very significant growth in revenue: ideal for
T
potential GEM investors. The prospectus further revealed the following,
T
namely, that –
U U
- 5 -
V V

A A
a. The revenue of the Paprika Group as a whole had increased by
B B
57.8% from $56,980,000 in the year ended 31 March 2016 to
$89,894,000 in the year ended 31 March 2017.
C C
b. Retail sales had made up 90.5% and 88.5% of the group’s
D D
revenue in the two years of the track record.
E E
c. Although wholesale trade made up just 10% of the Group’s
F F
revenue, that trade had, in fact, increased by 96% from
$5.1 million to $10 million.
G G
d. Concerning cash proceeds, they would be deposited into the
H H
Group’s designated bank accounts as soon as reasonably
I I
practicable after the relevant date of sale. Cash pending delivery
to the bank for deposit would be kept in safes located in each
J J
retail store.
K K
20. The listing application further revealed that three of the Paprika
L L
Group’s top five suppliers, accounting for 53.4% of the Group’s total
M purchases in the year ended 31 March 2017, were new suppliers: corporations M
which had only commenced doing business with the Paprika Group in 2016.
N N
This included API Trading Company Limited.
O O
21. More particularly, the listing application stated that, in respect
P P
of wholesale revenue, over 90% of the increase in sales had been attributable
Q
to purchases made by a single independent third party, a company called Novi
Q
eBusiness Limited.
R R
S 22. As it was, upon consideration of the listing application, both the S
SFC and the Stock Exchange expressed concerns as to the accuracy and/or
T T
reliability of the declared earnings contained in the two-year track record
U U
- 6 -
V V

A A
period. In respect of these concerns, between July 2017 and January 2018
B B
RaffAello, as sponsor, conducted further due diligence, made a number of
written submissions and, in addition, filed six further revised proofs of the
C C
prospectus.
D D
23. On 19 December 2017, due to the lapse of more than six months
E E
from the date on which the application for listing had been filed, Paprika was
F F
required to re-submit the listing application. This it did.
G G
24. Eventually, however, on 16 April 2018, with queries still to be
H resolved, the listing application was formally withdrawn. The application has H
not been resuscitated.
I I
J
The institution of disciplinary proceedings by the SFC
J
K 25. Some three years after this abandonment, in terms of a K
document dated 11 June 2021, the SFC served on the Applicant a Notice of
L L
Proposed Disciplinary Action pursuant to section 194 of the Ordinance. It was
M said in the notice that the SFC was of the preliminary view that RaffAello, in M
discharging its obligations as sponsor, had been guilty of ‘misconduct’ in that
N N
it had failed to perform all reasonable due diligence in respect of the Paprika
O
Group before submitting the listing application and accordingly was not fit
O
and proper to remain licensed.
P P
Q 26. ‘Misconduct’, as alleged by the SFC, is defined in section 193 Q
of the Ordinance as, inter alia, an act or omission relating to the carrying on
R R
of any regulated activity for which a person is licensed or registered which, in
S
the opinion of the Commission, (that is, the SFC) is, or is likely to be,
S
prejudicial to the interests of the investing public.
T T
U U
- 7 -
V V

A A
27. It was the SFC case, therefore, that RaffAello’s ‘misconduct’,
B B
that is, its asserted failure to conduct all reasonable due diligence, was to be
judged as at the date of filing of that application, that is, as at 14 June 2017,
C C
this being the date at which, for all essential purposes, the integrity of the
D D
listing application had to be judged.
E E
28. It was the contention of the SFC that RaffAello’s culpability,
F F
that is, its failure to conduct reasonable due diligence, was to be judged within
the context of two issues - which will be looked at in greater detail later -
G G
which may be described as follows -
H H
a. a failure to conduct adequate due diligence concerning retail
I I
sale transactions effected at Paprika retail outlets in Hong Kong;
J J
b. a failure to ascertain the true independence of two companies
K
that had reportedly conducted arm’s length dealings with the
K
Paprika Group during the two-year track record period as well
L L
as their true commercial substance, these companies (already
M M
mentioned above) being: Novi eBusiness Limited, the largest
wholesale purchaser of merchandise from the Group, and API
N N
Trading Company Limited, a major supplier of merchandise to
O O
the Group.
P P
29. The assertion made was that the culpability contained within the
Q two areas set out above had been brought about by a failure on the part of Q
RaffAello, prior to the filing of Paprika’s listing application, to examine with
R R
professional scepticism the accuracy and completeness of statements and
S representations made, or other information given to it in the course of the due S
diligence exercise.
T T
U U
- 8 -
V V

A A
30. Put simply, that, prior to the filing of the listing application,
B B
during the course of exercising due diligence, a number of concerning issues
had presented themselves to the RaffAello transaction team, these issues
C C
suggesting that fraudulent steps may have been taken to seek to give the
D D
impression of boosted revenues - in short, issues of real concern going to the
integrity of the listing - but those issues had either been missed or, if identified,
E E
had not been investigated with the required level of diligence, too much
F F
reliance being placed on Paprika’s own representations made through its own
management.
G G
H H
31. It was the case for the SFC that, if proved, this failure on the
part of RaffAello, relating to what should have been done by it prior to the
I I
filing of the listing application, constituted breaches of the Code of Conduct
J J
and paragraph 2 of Practice Note 2 of the Rules Governing the Listing of
Securities on the GEM Board (‘the GEM Listing Rules’). More specifically,
K K
it constituted breaches of the following -
L L
a. Paragraphs 17.2(b) and 17.4(a) (reasonable due diligence) by
M M
failing to perform all reasonable due diligence on Paprika before
N N
submitting the listing application to the Stock Exchange.
O O
b. Paragraph 17.6(b) (professional scepticism) by failing to
examine with professional scepticism the accuracy and
P P
completeness of information/documents provided by Paprika
Q Q
and being alert to information that contradicted or brought into
question the reliability of such information/documents.
R R
c. Paragraph 17.6(c) (appropriate verification) by failing to
S S
undertake additional due diligence to ascertain the truth and
T T
completeness of the information provided by Paprika after
U RaffAello became aware of circumstances that cast doubt on the U
- 9 -
V V

A A
information provided to it or otherwise indicated a potential
B B
problem or risk.
C
d. Paragraph 17.6(f) (interview practices) by failing to identify any
C
irregularities noted during the interviews with the wholesaler,
D D
Novi eBusiness Limited, and supplier of products, API Trading
E E
Company Limited, and to ensure any irregularities noted during
the interviews were adequately explained and resolved.
F F
e. General Principle 7 (Compliance) and paragraph 12.1
G G
(Compliance: in general) by failing to comply with all
H H
regulatory requirements applicable to the conduct of its business
activities so as to promote the best interest and the integrity of
I I
the market.
J J
32. As to the issue of sanctions, RaffAello was informed that the
K K
SFC was of the preliminary view that, by way of sanction, it would be
L appropriate to issue a press release publicly reprimanding RaffAello and, in L
addition, to fine it a sum of $13 million.
M M
N 33. RaffAello sought to contest the preliminary findings of N
misconduct, both as to culpability and the imposition of sanctions.
O O
Correspondence then followed with RaffAello seeking to persuade the SFC
P that it had not discharged its obligations as sponsor in a culpable manner. P
Q Q
34. In a final Decision Notice dated 8 May 2023, however, the SFC
R stood by its preliminary decisions concerning culpability. R
S S
35. As to sanction, the SFC no longer insisted that the Applicant
T was not a fit and proper person to remain licensed, conceding that, in the T
circumstances of the case, while it would remain licensed, there should still
U U
- 10 -
V V

A A
be a public reprimand. Having regard to RaffAello’s submissions as to its
B B
difficult financial circumstances, it was ordered that the fine to be imposed
should be reduced to HK$4 million.
C C
D 36. RaffAello then applied (out of time2) pursuant to section 217 of D
the Ordinance for a review of the SFC decision, both as to the findings against
E E
it of culpability and the determinations related to sanction.
F F
The role of this Tribunal
G G
37. It is now well settled that applications for review before this
H H
Tribunal are not to be determined as an appeal of the classic kind is to be
I determined by a court of appeal. Instead, applications for review before this I
Tribunal must be determined de novo, that is, as a full merits review with the
J J
Tribunal acting as if it is the original decision maker. It does not mean that it
K
ignores what has gone before. It does mean, however, that it exercises its
K
independent judgement, arriving at its own decision. It does so in respect of
L L
liability and, if required, in respect of appropriate sanctions too.
M M
The burden and standard of proof
N N
38. The burden of proof remains on the SFC. Accordingly, it
O O
remains for the SFC to prove its case. As for the standard of proof, the civil
P P
standard, that is, proof on the balance of probabilities, applies.
Q Q
The dynamics of due diligence
R R
39. The nature and extent of the concept of ‘due diligence’ has been
S
considered by this Tribunal on a number of occasions. In this regard, in Sun
S
T T
2
The Chairman of the Tribunal heard arguments as to whether RaffAello should be permitted to proceed
even though its application was filed out of time. Leave was granted as was an application to file expert
evidence.
U U
- 11 -
V V

A A
Hung Kai International v SFC3 , it was said -
B B
“It is clear to us that the regulatory framework insisting on the exercise of
C due diligence by each and every sponsor is critical to the orderly and C
transparent working of the market. That is why emphasis is placed on the
dual obligation of a sponsor, an obligation not only to the client but,
D D
equally importantly, to the integrity of the market. In an orderly and
transparent market investors must be able to place trust, first, in the fact
E that the listing of a company has been founded by the Stock Exchange on E
the consideration of full and accurate information and, second, that the
information contained in the prospectus is also full and accurate. The route
F F
to ensuring such trust must rest principally on the sponsor, the party
responsible for the management of the listing. It must rest principally on
G the conduct of an objective, professional and scrupulous investigation of G
all material relevant to the listing and the initial public offering; in short,
on the conduct of reasonable due diligence.”
H H
I 40. As the Tribunal continued - I
J J
“All investment involves risk. The point is that investors must be able to
assess the risk by relying on accurate and relevant information. If they are
K unable to do that then trust in the market is undermined.” K
L L
41. Looking more closely at the role of sponsors, in Yi Shun Da
M Capital Limited v SFC4 the Tribunal emphasised that - M
N N
“… in undertaking its role, a sponsor must examine the accuracy and
completeness of representations made to it by the representatives of the
O company seeking listing and do so with the necessary degree of O
professional scepticism. The requirement to adopt an attitude of
professional scepticism places on the shoulders of a sponsor the
P P
requirement to make a critical assessment of relevant information, doing
so with a questioning mind and – of central importance in the present case
Q – being alert to information that brings into question the reliability of Q
relevant statements and representations.”
R R
42. The Tribunal recognises, of course, that a sponsor is not
S S
required to provide counsel of perfection. It also recognises that, viewed
T T
3
Application No. 3 of 2013, 27 January 2014.
U 4 Application No. 4 of 2020, 19 October 2021. U
- 12 -
V V

A A
through the more leisurely prism of hindsight, it may unconsciously be too
B B
easy to elevate reasonable due diligence to a level that requires unassailable
diligence. The Tribunal recognises therefore that there will be cases when,
C C
even if reasonable due diligence is carried out, not every concern will be
D D
identified and dealt with. The method by which a sponsor discharges its dual
obligation is by employing reasonable skill and care, tempered always with a
E E
healthy but rational degree of scepticism, doing so in the best interest of its
F F
client and – importantly – the integrity of the market.
G G
43. Paragraph 17.4 of the Code of Conduct provides that -
H H
“Where the sponsor becomes aware of circumstances that may cast doubt
I
on information provided to it or otherwise indicate a potential problem or
I
risk, the sponsor should undertake additional due diligence to ascertain the
truth and completeness of the matter and information concerned.”
J J
K K
44. Of particular importance in this present matter is the fact that, if
there is a requirement placed on the sponsor to make a critical assessment of
L L
particular information, most certainly of information that presents potential
M M
concerns, there is a need in the sponsor’s due diligence records to make a note
of what has been done. If issues of concern are identified, it is not sufficient
N N
for the sponsor simply to investigate the matter, make a bald note of that fact,
O O
if making any note at all, and to move on. It is to be remembered that the
sponsor is not the only guardian of the public interest in the listing of a
P P
corporation. All listing applications are scrutinised by the SFC and the Stock
Q Q
Market itself and those authorities need to be assured that matters of concern
- ‘red flags’5 - have been identified by the sponsor and given the necessary
R R
S S
T 5
‘Red flags’ are a posting of concern, including apprehension of the fraudulent manufacture of sales figures
T
and the like in order to artificially boost revenue and make a corporation seeking listing look more
profitable than in truth it is. Their true nature and extent must be identified and dealt with and, if that is
U not possible, the existing risk must be made known to the market. U
- 13 -
V V

A A
level of consideration.
B B
45. This is not to suggest that the sponsor must write an exhaustive
C C
report of everything that is stumbled upon in its due diligence records. But it
D is to suggest that, if a matter of concern is identified by a sponsor in the course D
of the exercise of due diligence, a coherent note should be made of what has
E E
been discovered and what has been resolved.
F F
46. In the present case, for an extended period after the filing of the
G G
application for listing, exchanges took place between the sponsor and the SFC,
H those exchanges essentially being aimed at attempting to resolve concerns that H
had in many instances, either in whole or in part, been identified by the
I I
sponsor prior to the listing application being made. Coherent notes on the part
J
of the sponsor made at the time due diligence was carried out would no doubt
J
have resolved those concerns in a short period of time and at far lesser cost.
K K
L 47. Perhaps the guidance most directly relevant to the present L
matter is to be found in paragraph 17.4 of the Code of Conduct which provides
M M
that -
N N
“Where the sponsor becomes aware of circumstances that may cast doubt
O
on information provided to it or otherwise indicate a potential problem or
O
risk, the sponsor should undertake additional due diligence to ascertain the
truth and completeness of the matter and information concerned.”
P P
Q
48. Importantly, that paragraph concludes by saying -
Q
R R
“Over reliance on management representations or confirmations for the
purpose of verifying information received from a listing applicant cannot
S S
be regarded as reasonable due diligence.”
T T
U U
- 14 -
V V

A A
Looking to the core issues in dispute
B B
49. The essential assertion made by the SFC, the burden of proof
C being on it, is that RaffAello failed to examine with the required level of skill C
and professional scepticism the accuracy and completeness of statements and
D D
representations made, or other information given to it by management and
E staff of the Paprika Group, and further failed to be alert to information that E
contradicted or brought into question the reliability of those representations.
F F
In short, that there was, in the circumstances prevailing, a lack of due
G
diligence.
G
H H
50. The core issue for determination, therefore, is the adequacy of
I the due diligence conducted by RaffAello; whether it met the standard of I
rigour required of it by the Code of Conduct and relevant regulatory
J J
provisions or whether it has been demonstrated that it fell below that standard.
K K
51. Although, during the course of argument, assertions were made
L L
that certain transactions falling for scrutiny may have pointed to fraudulent
M steps being taken to seek to boost revenue figures, proof of this has never been M
integral to the SFC case. There has therefore been no burden placed on the
N N
SFC to demonstrate that the application for listing made by Paprika was, in
O
fact, to a greater or lesser degree, built on manufactured figures.
O
P P
52. The burden placed on the SFC has been to demonstrate, first,
Q
that, on a consideration of all relevant material, matters of concern should
Q
have been raised as to the integrity of the listing application and, second, in
R R
light of that concern, and the need generally for a sceptical approach, that there
S S
was a failure by the RaffAello transaction team to conduct reasonable due
diligence before the submission of the listing application to the Stock
T T
Exchange and the SFC.
U U
- 15 -
V V

A A
The SFC’s underlying assertions of culpability
B B
53. As to the broad, underlying allegations of culpability made
C against RaffAello by the SFC, these have included the following - C
D D
a. RaffAello failed to take due diligence measures that would get
E to the bottom of the red flags. Conducting interviews with the E
management of the Paprika Group and sales staff, and third
F F
parties, would plainly not suffice. RaffAello placed substantial
G emphasis on the fact that it verified the existence of the sales G
and supply transactions (namely, that the transactions actually
H H
had happened and money actually had been exchanged). But
I that missed the point. It was not the existence of the transactions I
per se that mattered but whether the transactions had been
J J
genuine commercial transactions or in fact manufactured.
K K
b. RaffAello all too often ‘went through the motions’, its
L L
interviews being perfunctory and all too often devoid of any
attempt to understand the true dynamics of matters. Put another
M M
way, it failed to adopt an attitude of professional scepticism and
N N
to make a critical assessment of all relevant information.
O O
c. RaffAello, it seems, at least during this application for review,
placed substantial weight on the fact that it had been supported
P P
in its due diligence by the reporting accountants and, to a much
Q Q
lesser degree, by its lawyers. This, however, fundamentally
misunderstood the regulatory imperative that it was RaffAello
R R
itself which was principally responsible for the accuracy of the
S S
contents of the prospectus.
T T
U U
- 16 -
V V

A A
Assertions underlying RaffAello’s case
B B
54. Ms. Cindy Kong, leading counsel for the Applicant, RaffAello,
C in her very vigorous and full set of submissions, set out a number of C
fundamental contentions which, she argued, proved that the SFC, in its very
D D
extended review of RaffAello’s due diligence, had elevated the requirement
E for reasonable due diligence to a requirement for unassailable due diligence. E
As she put it, the SFC had built its case “on nothing but criticisms based on
F F
hindsight and taken out of context”; in short, in her contention, the SFC had
G
based its case on criticisms divorced from the reality of how due diligence
G
exercises must, in practice, be conducted.
H H
I 55. In this regard, the Tribunal accepts, of course, that it must I
consider matters within the context of reality by having regard to what was
J J
known to the sponsor at the time or what should reasonably, in the
K
circumstances prevailing at the time, have been further investigated by the
K
sponsor.
L L
M a. Discretion M
N 56. Ms. Kong submitted that, in light of the fact that every exercise N
of due diligence will have to meet the challenge of new facts, new
O O
circumstances and new business imperatives, sponsorship teams had to be
P given significant discretion, this including a discretion to determine in good P
faith whether, on information obtained and assessed, explanations should be
Q Q
accepted as being reasonable or whether, in all the circumstances, there should
R
be further investigation. That, of course, is correct. Sponsorship is not a book-
R
marking exercise. Each and every application for listing will present its own
S S
issues. But, of course, it also follows that a discretion to act does not excuse a
T T
failure to act when that failure, judged objectively, is culpable.
U U
- 17 -
V V

A A
b. Proportionality
B B
57. Ms. Kong also sought to support her submissions with an
C argument as to proportionality. As it was put by her, many of the issues in this C
listing related to a small proportion of Paprika’s total sales. In the context of
D D
the listing application as a whole, these issues, she said, would never have

Summary of findings
O O
222. The first broad issue that fell for determination in this matter
P P
was focused on whether RaffAello, in its role of sponsor, had failed to conduct
Q Q
reasonable due diligence in respect of transactions conducted at Paprika’s
retail outlets in Hong Kong. This broad issue encompassed three sub-issues
R R
which have been described in the body of this determination as follows -
S S
a. the issue of consecutive cash transactions;
T T
b. the issue of credit card bulk purchases, and
U U
- 64 -
V V

A A
c. the issue of the POS test invoices.
B B
223. In respect of each of these sub-issues, the Tribunal has found
C C
RaffAello to be culpable of a lack of due diligence.
D D
224. The second broad issue that fell for determination in this matter
E E
was focused on whether RaffAello, in its role of sponsor, had failed to conduct
F reasonable due diligence in seeking to determine whether two companies that F
(supposedly) had substantial dealings with Paprika were themselves
G G
companies of commercial substance and, in conducting business relationships
H with Paprika, had done so as independent third parties. In respect of each of H
the two companies, Novi and API, the Tribunal has found as follows -
I I
J a. as to the issue of whether each company, in conducting business J
dealings, had done so as an independent third-party, the
K K
Tribunal found that RaffAello had been culpable of a lack of
L due diligence. L
M b. as to the issue of their commercial substance, in respect of Novi, M
the Tribunal was of the opinion that it had not been
N N
demonstrated that there had been a failure of due diligence on
O the part of RaffAello. In respect of API, however, the Tribunal O
was satisfied that RaffAello had been culpable of a lack of due
P P
diligence.

transactions’, a fine of $4 million;
E E
b. in respect of the failures related to the issue of ‘credit card bulk
F F
purchases’, a fine of $4 million;
G G
c. in respect of the failures related to the issue of ‘POS Test
H
Invoices’, a fine of $2 million;
H
I I
d. in respect of the failures related to API and Novi, a fine of
$3 million.
J J
K 226. In seeking to contest the SFC’s provisional assessment, it was K
(among other matters) emphasised on behalf of RaffAello that it was a small
L L
corporate finance house that had incurred losses between 2018 and 2021 and
M that, importantly, it was able to show by way of financial statements prepared M
by its accountants that, as at December 2022, its total asset values stood at just
N N
$6.35 million. That being the case, the proposed fine of $13 million would
O render the company insolvent, putting it out of business. The consequences O
would be deeply prejudicial to ongoing sponsor engagements and would, of
P P
course, severely impact the livelihood of the company’s employees.
Q Q
227. Having considered the submissions made on behalf of
R R
RaffAello, while the SFC did not accept that its proposed fine of $13 million
S had been wrong in principle or at odds with the facts known to it at the time, S
it nevertheless determined that, in light of RaffAello’s particular financial
T T
circumstances, the fine should be reduced to one of $4 million.
U U
- 66 -
V V

A A
228. Of importance, there was no longer a finding that RaffAello was
B B
not fit and proper to remain licensed. There was therefore no suspension of its
licence which meant that, while subject to public sanction (by way of a
C C
published reprimand), it would nevertheless be able to continue its business
D D
operations.
E E
229. Before this Tribunal, however, it was argued on behalf of
F F
RaffAello that, in light of all relevant circumstances, particularly what were
submitted to be - at worst, - marginal levels of culpability, even this fine of $4
G G
million (coupled with a public reprimand) was wrong in principle and was
H H
excessive, placing the continued existence of the company in jeopardy.
I I
230. It was submitted that, for a relatively small company filling a
J
particular niche in the market, a public reprimand was itself a penalty of
J
substance which could, and would, no doubt have a profound effect on
K K
RaffAello’s ability to obtain future sponsorship mandates.
L L
B. A preliminary issue - revisiting RaffAello’s financial circumstances
M M
231. In final submissions made to the Tribunal, Mr. Nip, for the SFC,
N N
sought to revisit the issue of RaffAello’s parlous financial standing, the issue
O which had persuaded the SFC to reduce its fine from $13 million to O
$4 million.
P P
Q 232. It was Mr. Nip’s submission that, on a further study of all Q
relevant financial papers, it was now evident that deliberate steps had been
R R
taken by RaffAello to create the false appearance that a proposed financial
S penalty of any substance would place it in financial jeopardy. Mr. Nip’s S
submissions were founded on the provision contained in the SFC Disciplinary
T T
Fining Guidelines that, in assessing any financial sanction, the SFC may take
U U
- 67 -
V V

A A
into account the fact that deliberate steps had been taken to create such a false
B B
appearance.
C C
233. It was Mr. Nip’s submission that, on the evidence, the single,
D compelling inference to be drawn was that Ricky Tsang, who had acquired D
full interest in the company in mid-2018, once he knew his company was
E E
under investigation, had made a number of loans of substance, particularly a
F F
loan to his own sister’s company, all of these loans proving impossible to
recover. Indeed, his own sister’s company had subsequently gone into
G G
liquidation.
H H
234. Mr. Nip based his submissions on RaffAello’s relevant financial
I I
returns made to the SFC which, he argued, showed that from June 2021
J
RaffAello had made ‘substantial loans to associated companies’ which were
J
subsequently written off from December 2023 and thereafter.
K K
L 235. Ms. Kong, on behalf of RaffAello, strongly opposed any L
suggestion that any of the constrained assets of RaffAello had been
M M
deliberately transferred, hidden or disposed of in order to avoid an appropriate
N
financial penalty being imposed by the SFC. It was, she submitted, an
N
assertion which had been unfairly sprung on her client.
O O
P 236. The matter that needed to be demonstrated by Mr. Nip, of P
course, was one of deliberate, that is, intentional, hiding of funds. The mere
Q Q
fact that poor business decisions had been made, including ill-advised loans,
R
would not of themselves be sufficient unless they gave rise to the compelling
R
inference that they had been made in order to avoid the probable surrender of
S S
those funds at a later time to the SFC.
T T
237. Ms. Kong, on behalf of RaffAello, accepted, of course, that the
U U
- 68 -
V V

A A
company had undergone severe financial difficulties over the past few years
B B
and that, financially, it remained in a very difficult situation. It was her
submission, however, that the company’s accounts clearly showed that, due
C C
largely to the Covid crisis and its resulting financial stresses, a number of
D D
RaffAello’s Mainland clients had been unable to pay fees. In the result, in
2023, most of these fees had to be written off. Expressed broadly, it was
E E
Ms. Kong’s submission that prevailing difficulties in the market had been
F F
principally responsible for RaffAello’s dire financial problems and that there
was no evidence of any real weight that dishonest steps had been taken to hide
G G
assets.
H H
238. It was also pointed out by Ms. Kong that, in light of the
I I
seriousness of the allegations made, it was concerning that her client had not
J J
been cross examined in any depth as to the issue.
K K
239. In the opinion of the Tribunal, while the matters placed before
L L
the Tribunal by Mr. Nip as to the movement of funds did raise a level of
concern17, and may well have pointed to the exercise of poor business skills,
M M
it was not possible for the Tribunal, on a consideration of all the evidence
N N
placed before it during the hearing, to conclude that either Ricky Tsang, or
any of those employed by him in RaffAello, must have taken deliberate steps
O O
to place funds beyond the reach of the SFC.
P P
C. General principles relevant to assessing a financial penalty
Q Q
240. As the Tribunal understands it, there was no disagreement of
R R
substance between the parties as to the relevant considerations in determining
S
an appropriate sanction; these considerations being underpinned by the need
S
T T
17
Particularly regarding a substantial loan made to a company controlled by Ricky Tsang’s sister, a
company in financial difficulties at the time the loan was made.
U U
- 69 -
V V

A A
at all times to maintain and promote confidence in the Hong Kong securities
B B
and futures industry. In the judgment of the Tribunal, that must be
fundamental. Any material loss of confidence places the entire industry in
C C
peril.18
D D
241. In respect of the imposition of a financial penalty, the SFC
E E
Disciplinary Fining Guidelines must be taken in consideration. In the view of
F F
the Tribunal, having regard to the circumstances of this matter, the following
provisions are of particular relevance -
G G
H H
Whether the conduct in question was intentional, reckless or negligent
I 242. It was at all times accepted that RaffAello’s culpability was I
founded on the negligence only of its officers. There was no suggestion that it
J J
had acted intentionally to somehow deceive; no assertion of bad faith was
K
demonstrated. Nor was it demonstrated that any of the officers of the company
K
had acted recklessly in the discharge of their duties of due diligence.
L L
Accordingly, culpability had to be assessed at the lowest scale of the three
M M
forms listed, namely, on the basis of negligent conduct.
N N
243. Negligence itself, of course, has divisions of seriousness. The
O
negligence displayed in this case, in the view of the Tribunal (as earlier set
O
out), lay essentially in a failure to act with professional scepticism and a
P P
questioning mind.
Q Q
R R
S S
18
Counsel for both the Applicant and the SFC accepted the correctness of the dicta in Chu Kwok Shing
Godwin v SFC (SFAT No.1/2009, 30 June 2010; namely, that the purposes of disciplinary sanctions are,
T
first, punishment; second, deterrence; third, where suspension, revocation or prohibition is involved, to
T
ensure that the offender does not have the opportunity to repeat the offence, either for a limited period or
indefinitely and finally, and fundamentally, to maintain and promote confidence in the securities and
U futures industry. U
- 70 -
V V

A A
Whether the conduct in question caused loss to the market
B B
244. In assessing an appropriate penalty, the Tribunal considers it to
C be of importance that no loss was caused to market users or, more generally, C
to the market itself. After working with the Stock Market and the SFC over an
D D
extended period of time in order to try and put the application for listing into
E acceptable form, RaffAello withdrew the application. It has never been E
resubmitted. There was, therefore, no loss to any market participants nor any
F F
imminent danger that such a loss might occur.
G G
Whether the conduct in question damaged the integrity of the securities and
H H
futures market
I I
245. This again is a matter of importance in determining penalty.
There was, of course, potential damage to the integrity of the market. The
J J
Tribunal accepts, however, that good faith steps were taken by RaffAello to
K K
try and put its application for listing into good order but that, once it became
apparent that there were simply too many underlying obstacles, the
L L
application was withdrawn by it.
M M
N Whether the conduct under scrutiny is widespread in the industry N
246. While acts of negligence in the sponsorship process do arise
O O
from time to time, those acts being so significantly material that they warrant
P P
some form of sanction, nothing was put before the Tribunal to suggest that
there was any call for industry-wide concern prompting deterrent action in this
Q Q
particular case.
R R
S Whether there were sufficient guiding materials S
247. As the Tribunal has indicated earlier in this determination, over
T T
the years there has been ample guidance as to the nature and extent of due
U U
- 71 -
V V

A A
diligence and the particular importance of the role and responsibilities of a
B B
sponsor in that process. The RaffAello transaction team could not therefore
have been ignorant of the principles under which they were obliged to act.
C C
D 248. That said, it was submitted on behalf of RaffAello that such D
guidance as was available at the time “was relatively open ended”, leaving
E E
significant room for professional judgment and exercise of discretion. It was
F F
argued that this “lack of clear guidance and bright-line rules [must] make any
breach by RaffAello less egregious”. The Tribunal does not accept this
G G
submission. It is impossible to give guidelines which will clearly, without any
H H
need to use discretion, be able to make provision for each and every
circumstance which may arise. Guidance can be - and is – given. Thereafter,
I I
having digested the true meaning and impact of that guidance, it is for the
J J
sponsorship team, acting together, to ensure that the guidance is properly
understood and followed in respect of individual factual circumstances that
K K
will always arise in any exercise of sponsorship.
L L
249. As it was put by Mr. Nip (for the SFC) in the final written
M M
submissions of March 2025: “this is not a case which turns on resolving a
N N
particular ambiguity as to what the standards require.” The Tribunal agrees. It
has been instead a case of understanding the guiding principles, as they have
O O
(over the years) been set out in straightforward language, and ensuring, in an
P P
exercise of due diligence, that those principles are followed in the exercise of
due diligence.
Q Q
R R
D. Assessing the amount of a financial sanction
S
250. This has not been the easiest decision to make. It is an
S
underlying principle that, in assessing a penalty, one of the principal matters
T T
to be taken into account encompasses a consideration of the financial
U U
- 72 -
V V

A A
circumstances of the party subject to that penalty. By way of illustration, a
B B
fine of $20 million may be of little, if any consequence, to a major investment
bank (far less wounding than the blow to its reputation) but it may be
C C
devastating to a small, that is, a ‘boutique’ sponsorship company such as the
D D
Applicant.
E E
251. It is not disputed that RaffAello - which has comparatively
F F
always been a smaller company, looking, it seems, to the more modest end of
the sponsorship market - is at this time a company under very considerable
G G
financial stress. The SFC itself took into account that too great a financial
H H
penalty would (in all probability) simply drive the company into liquidation,
prejudicing any sponsorship work in the hands of the company and, of course,
I I
in all likelihood, meaning that the current employees would be out of work.
J J
Hence the reduction of its financial penalty from $13 million to $4 million.
K K
252. There is an important place for smaller companies in the market.
L L
The very existence of the GEM market underscores the point.
M M
253. Accordingly, when considering an appropriate financial penalty
N
as a form of punishment, the ability to meet that penalty without going out of
N
business, while not in every instance determinative, must always be a
O O
persuasive factor. In this regard, the Tribunal is of the same view as the SFC,
P P
namely, that - remembering that fraud or reckless conduct was not identified,
nor was there loss to market participants - too stern a financial penalty may
Q Q
simply drive RaffAello into liquidation, giving it no options for survival. It
R R
may also send a wrong signal to other smaller companies in the financial
market.
S S
T T
254. That said, of course, although not applicable in the present case,
the Tribunal accepts that, there will be cases when the degree of culpability is
U U
- 73 -
V V

A A
such that the principle of deterrence must be given full recognition. There may
B B
well be occasions, therefore, when the risk of liquidation must simply be
accepted.
C C
D 255. In the present case, looking broadly to the full range and depth D
of the Applicant’s failings in this matter in the discharge of its important
E E
mandate, one that seeks to protect the investing public and, of particular
F F
importance, one that must also protect the reputation of the financial industry
in Hong Kong, the Tribunal determined that a somewhat sterner financial
G G
penalty than the one imposed by the SFC, namely, a financial penalty of $4
H H
million, would have been appropriate. In this regard, the Tribunal would have
looked to a penalty of between $4.5 and $5 million.
I I
J
256. That said, the Tribunal has also had to take into account the fact
J
that it came to a determination that RaffAello bore no culpability in respect of
K K
one of the matters for which it had been found liable originally by the SFC.
L L
257. In light of that finding, bearing in mind that RaffAello will be
M M
subject to a public reprimand, one that will no doubt undermine its reputation,
N
the Tribunal is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, taking into account the
N
various matters urged upon it by counsel for both the SFC and RaffAello, and
O O
at all times recognising that it must determine penalty de novo, that is, as if it
P P
is the original decision maker, the Tribunal has determined that a financial
penalty of $4 million, the same amount determined by the SFC would best
Q Q
meet the ends of justice in this matter.
R R
258. The Tribunal has given anxious consideration to whether or not
S S
there should be an order of suspension for some period of time. However,
T T
along with the SFC, the Tribunal does not consider that in the present case it
is required, more especially as a public sanction is to be published.
U U
- 74 -
V V

Part 2 - The Law
B B
The nature of the review
C C
31. There is no dispute that the review of the Commission’s decisions, conducted
pursuant to section 218 of the Ordinance, is a hearing de novo and is to be conducted as a full
D D
merits review, including the determination in respect of sanctions, if any, to be imposed on
Mr. Choi.33
E E
The burden of proof
F F
32. Given that the review is a hearing de novo, the burden of proof remains on the
G Commission to prove that Mr. Choi is not a fit and proper person for the purposes of the G
Ordinance.
H H
The standard of proof
I I
33. As provided by section 218(7) of the Ordinance the standard of proof required
to determine any question or issue before the Tribunal is the standard of proof applicable to
J J
civil proceedings in a court of law. That is, matters must be proved on a balance of probability.
K K
Inferences
L L
34. In drawing of inferences, given the nature of these proceedings, the Tribunal
does not have to be satisfied that it is the only inference to be drawn from proved facts. That is
M M
required in criminal proceedings. Rather, in these circumstances the Tribunal has to be satisfied
N that that it has been established as a compelling inference.34 N
O Failure to call a witness O
35. Where a party without explanation fails to call as a witness a person who might
P P
reasonably be expected to give direct evidence on the matters in question it is permissible for
Q the court to draw adverse inferences. Q
R 36. There must be a reasonable basis for some hypothesis in the evidence or the R
inherent probabilities, before a court can draw useful inferences from a party’s failure to rebut
S S
T T
33 Tsien Pak Cheong David v Securities and Futures Commission [2011] 3 HKLRD 533.
34 HKSAR v Lee Ming Tee (2003) 6 HKCFAR 336; Sir Anthony Mason NPJ at paragraph 72.
U U
- 10 -
V V

A A
it.35 The issue is to be approached in the manner described by Lord Lowry in his judgment in
B the House of Lords in R v IRC, ex p. TC Coombs & Co36: B
“…the silence of one party in face of the other party’s evidence may convert that
C C
evidence into proof in relation to matters which are, or are likely to be, within
the knowledge of the silent party and about which that party could be expected
D to give evidence. Thus, depending on the circumstances, a prima facie case may D
become a strong or even an overwhelming case. But, if the silent party’s failure
to give evidence (or to give the necessary evidence) can be credibly explained,
E E
even if not entirely justified, the effect of his silence in favour of the other party
may be either reduced or nullified.”
F F
37. The applicable relevant principles are:37
G G
“(1) In certain circumstances a court may be entitled to draw adverse inferences
from the absence or silence of a witness who might be expected to have
H H
material evidence to give on an issue in an action.
I (2) If a court is willing to draw such inferences, they may go to strengthen the I
evidence adduced on that issue by the other party or to weaken the
evidence, if any, adduced by the party who might reasonably have been
J J
expected to call the witness.
K (3) There must, however, have been some evidence, however weak, adduced K
by the former on the matter in question before the court is entitled to draw
the desired inference: in other words, there must be a case to answer on
L that issue. L
(4) If the reason for the witness’s absence or silence satisfies the court, then
M M
no such adverse inference may be drawn. If, on the other hand, there is
some credible explanation given, even if it is not wholly satisfactory, the
N potentially detrimental effect of his/her absence or silence may be reduced N
or nullified.”
O O
Codes of Conduct
P P
38. Section 193(3) of the Ordinance provides in having regard to the issue of
misconduct and in forming an opinion whether an act or omission is or is likely to be prejudicial
Q Q
to the interest of the investing public or to the public interest, the Commission shall have regard
R R
S S
35 Prest v Petrodel Resources Ltd [2013] AC 415; Lord Sumption at paragraph 44.
T 36 R v IRC, ex p. TC Coombs & Co [1991] 2 AC 283, at page 300 F-H, cited with approval in the judgment of T
Kwan JA, as Kwan VP then, in Pacific Electric Wire & Cable Co Ltd v Texan Management Ltd (CACV
95/2012) 17 September 2013, at paragraph.107.
U 37 Wiszniewski v Central Manchester Health Authority [1998] PIQR 324, Brooke LJ at page 340, cited with U

A A
to any code of conduct published under section 169 and to guidelines published under section
B 399 of the Ordinance in force at the time of the occurrence and applicable to the act or omission. B
C C
39. The Code of Conduct for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities
and Futures Commission, published by the Commission in March 2014, is relevant as is the
D D
Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct, published by the Commission in October 2013.
E E
Fit and proper person
F 40. Sections 194(3) and 196(3) of the Ordinance provides that in determining F
whether a regulated person is a fit and proper person, within the meaning of those respective
G G
sections, the Commission:
H “ …may, among other matters (including those specified in section 129), take H
into account such present or past conduct of the regulated person as it considers
appropriate in the circumstances of the case”
I I
J 41. Section 129 provides that in making that determination, in addition to any other J
matter the Commission may consider relevant, the Commission shall have regard to the
K following in respect of the person: K
L “(a) the financial status or solvency; L
(b) the educational or other qualifications or experience having regard to the
M nature of the functions which, if the application is allowed, the person will M
perform;
N N
(c) the ability to carry on the regulated activity competently, honestly and
fairly; and
O O
(d) the reputation, character, reliability and financial integrity.”

K 320. Mr. Shieh submitted that under the general law a conflict of interest denotes a K
conflict between a fiduciary’s duty to his principal and his interest. If there is no duty, there is
L L
no conflict.332 The existence of the conflict was to be determined by the application of an

M M
possibility” of conflict.333

interest”, namely a “…personal concern of possible significant pecuniary value” in a
P transaction effected by a fiduciary. 334 P

A consideration of the submissions
401. In determining whether a conflict of interest arose for Mr. Choi in his dealings
D D
with UBS’s clients, the starting point of the analysis must be that he was a licensed person
E under the Ordinance. He had volunteered to obtain that status. In doing so, he made himself E
subject to the regulatory regime imposed under the Ordinance. Under the regime, the undoubted
F F
rights, privileges and benefits of being a licensed person are balanced by correlative duties.
G G
402. In my judgement, there is considerable merit in Mr. Li’s submission that the
SFC’s Code of Conduct and the CFA Code imposed freestanding duties on Mr. Choi to take
H H
reasonable steps to avoid conflicts of interest and to act in the best interests of UBS’s clients,
I affording them fair treatment. Clearly, in those circumstances a conflict of interest does not I
arise exclusively in the context of a fiduciary relationship, nor does it require that Mr. Choi
J J
have personal contractual duties to a client of UBS.
K K
403. I am satisfied that there is no reason why the Codes made under the Ordinance
or the UBS Guidelines and Policies could not impose broader duties, than those imposed by the
L L
general law, to (i) avoid conflicts of interest and (ii) require disclosure of such conflicts, actual
M or potential, that might arise. M
N 404. UBS’s Policies and Guidelines requiring disclosure to UBS of conflicts of N
interest with clients, including those arising in consequence of relevant personal relationships,
O O
were entirely consistent with the requirements of the SFC Code and the CFA Code requiring
that clients be treated fairly. Following appropriate disclosure, internal escalation within UBS
P P
afforded UBS the opportunity to consider its own interests and that of the client and to
determine whether it was proper to continue with the relationship and, if so, with what
Q Q
disclosure to the client and with what precautions. Obviously, the failure to make appropriate
R disclosure to UBS of a conflict of interest rendered nugatory the policy of escalation and R
considered determination of the issue of fair treatment of a client.
S S
T T
U U

410. As the Commission noted in its Decision Notice, section 193(1) of the

G G
“(d) an act or omission relating to the carrying on of any regulated activity for
H which a person is licensed or registered which, in the opinion of the Commission, H

“unless it has had regard to any code or guideline… as are in force at the time of occurrence of,
L and applicable in relation to, the act or omission”, as published under various provisions of the L
Ordinance. The Code of Conduct for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and
M M
Futures Commission and the Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct are two such relevant
codes.

R R

S Commission may consider relevant,… have regard to: S
“(c) the ability to carry on the regulated activity competently, honestly and fairly;

and

A consideration of the submissions
The construction of “relating to” in section 193(1)(d)
D D
432. On 1 June 2011, Moody’s Investor Services Hong Kong Limited (“Moody’s”)
E E
became licensed under the Ordinance to carry on Type 10 regulated activity, namely providing
credit rating services. On 11 July 2011, Moody’s published a ‘Special Comment Report’
F F
entitled: “Red Flags for Emerging-Market Companies: A Focus on China”. The Report received
G extensive local and international media attention and had a material impact on the market, in G
that the price, at which the shares of many of the companies the subject of the report traded,
H H
fell substantially following the publication of the Report.
I I
433. The Commission initiated proceedings, alleging and then finding that Moody’s
had failed to ensure the integrity of the Report. In the Court of Appeal, the Tribunal’s finding
J J
that the Report “…constituted a credit rating and was therefore a regulated activity” was
K rejected, although the Court of Appeal agreed with the alternative finding of the Tribunal that K
publication of the Report was “…an act or omission relating to the carrying on of a regulated
L activity within the ambit of s. 193(1)(d) of the Ordinance”. It was against that finding that L
Moody’s appealed to the Court of Final Appeal.
M M
434. In the Court of Final Appeal, Mr. Shieh submitted on behalf of Moody’s that:486
N N
“…in the context of s. 193(1)(d), the preparation and publication of a document
O such as the Report, which did not itself involve the provision of credit rating O
services , could only be said to “relat[e] to” the provision of such services if it
had been (or was understood, or would reasonably have been understood to have
P P
been) involved in the preparation of credit ratings. Unless such a clear and
limited meaning is given to the phrase, he contended, the law would be uncertain,
which is particularly inappropriate in the context of Pt. IX of the Ordinance,
Q Q
given that it creates an offence and involves curtailing freedom of expression.”
R R
435. The Court rejected that submission. Lord Neuberger said:487
S S
“35. This interpretation of the section involves giving the phrase “relating to” an
inappropriately narrow and specific effect. At any rate as a general proposition,

subject-matters to which the words refer” see the authorities cited by Fok PJ in

Conclusion
F F
460. I have no hesitation whatsoever in being satisfied that the conduct of Mr. Choi
and UBS in respect of the pre-IPO investment in Xinte related to the carrying on of the regulated
G G
activity, namely advising Xinte on securities in respect of its IPO. The contemporaneous
H documentation cited earlier speak eloquently to the fact that the funds raised in the pre-IPO H
investment were to be used for the benefit of the company in advance of the IPO. Clearly, that
I I
was intimately connected with and related to the latter project.

The applicability of the Codes of Conduct
K 461. The second question that arises is whether the Codes of Conduct, made pursuant K
to the provisions of the Ordinance, are applicable to conduct in the pre-IPO investment project.
L L
In his submissions, Mr. Shieh contended that they are not applicable.
M M
462. In the judgment of the Court of Appeal in Moody’s, 510 Lam VP, as Lam PJ was
then, noted that it was contended on behalf of Moody’s that the Code of Conduct published by
N N
the Commission, pursuant to section 169 (1) of the Ordinance “…could only apply to the
O carrying on of the regulated activities. Hence, there could not be a breach of the Code in respect O
of non-regulated activities.” That had been the Determination of the Securities and Futures
P Appeals Tribunal.511 P
Q Q
463. Of that submission, Lam VP said:
R “This is not a ground in the Notice of Appeal. In any event, once we reject the R
narrow construction of s. 193 as to the scope of the business of credit rating,
there is no merit in the argument.”
S S
T T
510 Moody’s Investors Services Hong Kong Limited v Securities and Futures Commission [2017] 3 HKLRD 565;
at page 577, paragraph 34-35.
U 511 Moody’s Investors Services Hong Kong Limited v Securities and Futures Commission SFAT No. 4 of 2014; U

A A
The issue was not addressed in the Court of Final Appeal.
B B
464. Although Lam VP’s observations were clearly obiter dicta, they clearly resonate
C C
with a broad interpretation of section 193 of the Ordinance. There is considerable force in
Mr. Li’s submission that, given that section 193(3) of the Ordinance expressly requires the
D D
Commission to have regard to Codes of Conduct, published under section 169 of the Ordinance,
E before forming an opinion that an act is prejudicial to the interest of the investing public or the E
public interest”, it would make little sense to construe the provision as being limited only to
F F
regulating conduct constituting the carrying on of the regulated activity, and not to conduct
related to it.512
G G
465. In the result, I am satisfied that, in determining whether Mr. Choi’s conduct in

the SFC not to apply for the grant of a licence, registration or other approval or
T T
consent for a period of up to 3 years;
U U
- 189 -
V V

A A
(viii) he had no intention of applying for such regulatory approval in or outside Hong
B Kong; B
(ix) there was no victim of Mr. Choi’s conduct; UBS was not a victim of that conduct,
C C
given that it had allowed Mr. Choi to be unknowingly in a position of conflict;
D further it had allowed him to work in the pre-IPO work for Xinte without there D
being an engagement letter and to have completed his work in Project Frontier
E E
before an engagement letter was signed between the parties
F (x) the impact of Mr. Choi’s conduct on market integrity and the public interest was F
minimal;
G G
(xi) there was no evidence that the conduct was widespread in the industry;
H H
(xii) after the events Mr. Choi had developed his career, his business and the
securities and futures industry generally. Of his other contributions, Mr. Choi:
I I
(1) had committed substantial time and effort to the development of Hong
Kong’s capital markets in particular financial technology; (2) had cultivated
J J
talent and groomed future leaders in Hong Kong; (3) had supported charities;
K and (4) was the recipient of awards and honorary titles in recognition of those K
contributions;
L L
(xiii) Mr. Choi had a clean disciplinary record;
M M
(xiv) there had been delay of 5 ½ years in bringing the proceedings against Mr. Choi
after he had been first informed of the allegations, in consequence of which he
N N
is suffered substantial anxiety and stress;
O O
(xv) publicity generated by these proceedings had blighted Mr. Choi’s personal and
professional reputation. That was to be regarded as a form of sanction which he
P P
had already suffered.
Q Q
714. In the result, Mr. Shieh submitted that a prohibition of 24 months was manifestly
R excessive and disproportionate. Having regard to the various Statements of Disciplinary Action R
of the SFC and the Determinations of the Tribunal itself, to which he drew the Tribunal’s
S S
attention, he submitted that, in circumstances where no dishonesty was alleged, the appropriate
period of prohibition at most ought to be in the region of 9 to 12 months.
T T
U U
- 190 -
V V

A A
The Commission’s submissions
B B
715. In inviting the Tribunal to uphold the sanctions imposed by the Commission, Mr.
Li submitted that Mr. Choi’s misconduct had been committed over a substantial period of time,
C C
during which he occupied a senior position within UBS as a managing director and whilst he
D fulfilled the role of Project Sponsor and team leader in both projects. His involvement was D
extensive. The projects were substantial: the pre-IPO investment of LR Capital Growth I
E Company Limited in Xinte involved HK$500 million; and the sale of the selling shareholder’s E
shares in AMTD involved HK$1.14 billion.
F F
716. Mr. Li accepted that there was no evidence of any direct personal gain on Mr.
G G
Choi’s part. On the other hand, he suggested that the “surrounding circumstances” gave rise to
“grave suspicions as to Choi’s motives for behaving as he did”. Of those circumstances, he
H H
referred to: the ownership of shares of LR Capital of Ms. Amy Wong and Mr. Danny Choi; Mr.
I Choi’s relationship with Mr. Devon Fu; and that ultimately Mr. Choi became the chairman of I
AMTD.
J J
717. Mr. Choi’s conduct occasioned serious potential harm to UBS and put it at
K K
regulatory and reputational risk. Although the Commission had not taken disciplinary action
against UBS, that did not mean that UBS was not put at regulatory risk. UBS had been the
L L
subject of a lengthy investigation by the SFC, in which it was required to respond to numerous
M Notices of the Commission and, no doubt, required to expend substantial time and resources. M
N 718. Mr. Li took issue with the submission that there had been unjustified delay in N
commencing the disciplinary proceedings against Mr. Choi. Having been informed of the
O O
matter by UBS’s letter, dated 17 April 2017700, the Commission had initiated investigations,
issued multiple Notices and had sought to interview Mr. Choi on 8 December 2017. The
P P
Commission had been presented with the Davis Polk Report on 3 August 2018. Given the
Q volume of documentary material, in particular emails, it was not surprising that it took a Q
considerable time before proceedings were initiated against Mr. Choi, by the issue of the NPDA
R on 16 December 2020. R
S S
719. Issue was taken that Mr. Choi had been cooperative with the SFC. Mr. Choi had
refused to cooperate throughout.
T T
U U
700 Bundle 1, page 204.
- 191 -
V V

A A
720. Further, although it was accepted that no allegation was made expressly of
B dishonesty, Mr. Choi’s conduct was no technical breach. Rather, his assistance to LR Capital, B
in particular the forwarding of confidential information to Mr. Devon Fu, and his non-disclosure
C C
of his interest to UBS were deliberate decisions. Acting to favour one party over another was
an obvious risk of prejudice.
D D
721. Issue was taken with the submission that UBS bore responsibility for allowing
E E
Mr. Choi unknowingly to be put into a position of conflict. That was a startling submission.
F Given Mr. Choi’s failure to disclose his actual or potential conflicts of interest to UBS, it was F
not possible for UBS to consider the matter or put in place any measures to avoid and mitigate
G G
the conflict.
H H
722. Of the numerous decisions of the Commission and Determinations of this
Tribunal to which reference was made, Mr. Li reminded the Tribunal that all such cases were
I I
determined on their own facts. Further, that all the cases relied on by Mr. Shieh involved
J undisclosed personal securities trading by licensed individuals on public security markets. Such J
conduct was more readily detected compared to the conduct of Mr. Choi in a consideration of
K the submissions. K
L L
A consideration of the submissions
M 723. There is no dispute that Mr. Choi has, as it was described, “a clean disciplinary M
record”. Similarly, it is accepted that no financial loss was caused to UBS or its clients and that
N there was no financial gain to Mr. Choi. N
O O
724. Mr. Li did not take issue with Mr. Shieh’s submission that the impact of Mr.
Choi’s conduct upon market integrity or the public interest was minimal nor that there was no
P P
evidence that the misconduct of which he was culpable was widespread in the industry.
Similarly, no issue was taken with the submissions in respect of Mr. Choi’s conduct subsequent
Q Q
to the events the subject of these proceedings.
R R
725. The fact that Mr. Choi was culpable of the misconduct, which the Tribunal has
S found proved, whilst occupying a senior position in UBS is a factor of aggravation of his S
culpability. His seniority and the reputation it carried ought to have acted as a barrier to the
T T
temptation to act in the duplicitous manner in which he did, in particular passing on obviously
U U
- 192 -
V V

A A
confidential information to Mr. Devon Fu and, in Project Frontier, also actively assisting LR
B Capital in advancing its interests contrary to those of UBS’s client, the selling shareholders. B
C C
726. Although Mr. Choi was involved in the pre-IPO investment in Xinte from
August 2014 to April 2015, it is clear that the nub of his misconduct occurred in a shorter period
D D
of a few weeks from mid-February to mid-March 2015. Similarly, although Mr. Choi was
involved in respect of the sale of the selling shareholders’ shares in AMTD from March 2015
E E
until September/October 2015, it is clear that the nub of his misconduct again occurred in a
F shorter period of several weeks in May and June 2015. Nevertheless, taken together, the F
misconduct encompassed multiple, separate and distinct acts.
G G
727. I do not accept that in acting in a conflict of interest on the occasions he has
H H
been found to have so acted, that he did so in a ‘technical breach’ only. As noted earlier, I am
aware that Mr. Li acknowledged that the Commission has not alleged dishonesty against Mr.
I I
Choi. However, it is clear, as Mr. Li has submitted, that on numerous occasions acting in a
J conflict of interest he deliberately determined to pass on confidential information against the J
interests of the clients of UBS. That was not negligence. He intended to do that. Similarly, he
K repeatedly assisted LR Capital in drafting documents the object of which was to secure K
advantage for them in negotiations with the selling shareholders. In doing so, he acted in a
L L
conflict of interest. Again, that was deliberate. It was intentional. There is no evidence that Mr.
Choi sought prior advice from “advisors or supervisors”, of whom there were many within
M M
UBS.
N N
728. I am satisfied that there was a risk of potential harm to UBS in consequence of
O Mr. Choi’s conduct. One level of that risk was the institution of disciplinary proceedings against O
UBS by the Commission. That did not happen. However, another level of risk was that an
P P
intensive investigation was launched into UBS to determine whether it bore any culpability.
That did happen. In a Notice, dated 20 July 2017, the Commission informed UBS that it was
Q Q
“a person under investigation” and that, in consequence, it must provide the Commission with
R “records or documents and written answers”. UBS was informed that there was “reason to R
inquire” whether it and/or any persons connected with it “…is or was guilty of misconduct
S and/or is not fit and proper person” for the purposes of section 194 of the Ordinance. 701 There S
followed numerous other such Notices. As Mr. Li submitted, it is readily apparent that, as a
T T
U U
701 Bundle 2, pages 529-539.
- 193 -
V V

A A
consequence, in responding to regulatory demands, UBS were required to expend substantial
B time and resources. B
C C
729. I do not accept that Mr. Choi cooperated with the Commission’s investigation.
At the interview conducted of him by the SFC on 7 December 2017 he refused to answer
D D
questions. He was obliged to answer. The information supplied in the letter by his then solicitors
on 8 December 2017 addressed only some of the matters that had been raised with him in the
E E
oral interview the previous day. More to the point, in refusing to answer questions at the oral
F interview he had succeeded in avoiding the rigours of an oral interrogation. F
G 730. I am satisfied that there was no unjustified delay by the Commission in G
commencing proceedings against Mr. Choi by service on him of the NPDA on 16 December
H H
2020. Following the report of the matter by UBS to the Commission on 11 April 2017, it is
clear that the Commission actively pursued obtaining relevant information from multiple
I I
sources. That much is readily apparent from the 34 lever-arch files of documents served on the
J Tribunal by the Commission as the Hearing Bundle. It comprises over 11,800 pages. The J
electronic version of the Respondent’s Bundles of Evidence, filed in May 2022, also includes
K attachments to emails, which were not made part of the Hearing Bundle, and were not included K
in the hard copy of the Respondent’s Bundles of evidence filed in May 2022. The Commission
L L
said that was done “to save paper”. It comprises 28,806 pages of documents. Clearly, obtaining
the documentation and then arranging it in a form that was intelligible was a formidable task
M M
for the Commission. It is to be noted that in the written Representations made by Mr. Choi’s
N then solicitors on his behalf to the Commission, dated 16 April 2021, reference was made to N
the “sheer volume of documents available”: 33,000 documents in Project Oasis; and 23,000
O documents in Project Frontier.702 O
P P
731. No doubt, Mr. Choi has suffered an element of anxiety and stress, as a result of
the proceedings hanging over his head for many years after he was informed of the allegations.
Q Q
However, that is an inevitable consequence of the complications of such litigation. In addition,
R there is force in Mr. Li submission, in effect, that not having engaged or cooperated with the R
Commission it ill behoves Mr. Choi to pray that in aid of his mitigation. There is no evidence
S before the Tribunal of publicity adverse to Mr. Choi generated by the investigations into his S
T T
U U
702 Core Bundle, page 72, at paragraph 21.
- 194 -
V V

A A
conduct. In any event, if there was such publicity, and it contributed to his stress and anxiety,
B that is simply a consequence of investigations and litigation in a free society and open justice. B
C C
732. For the reasons I gave earlier, in addressing the submissions that it was irrational
of the Commission to issue an order of prohibition against Mr. Choi, I do not accept that
D D
submission that the voluntary undertaking articulated on behalf of Mr. Choi in his then
solicitor’s letter, dated 16 April 2021, “ …not to apply for a new licence, registration or other
E E
approval or consent for a period of up to 3 years” would have achieved the same practical effect,
F albeit for a longer period, as the prohibition imposed by the Commission. I accepted as correct, F
the response of the Commission in its Decision Notice to that suggestion: a formal disciplinary
G G
sanction serves to deter non-compliance with regulatory requirements.
H H
733. It is to be noted that in addressing the issue of the level of sanctions to be
imposed following disciplinary action in the pamphlet, Disciplinary Proceedings at a Glance,
I I
reference was made to other relevant factors including, “SFC’s action in previous similar cases
J (note: usually similar cases would be treated consistently…)” Of course, that is a simple J
articulation of a fundamental principle applicable to many activities and certainly applicable to
K these proceedings. However, it is trite that all cases occur in their individual circumstances. K
Whilst there are similarities with other cases, there are always dissimilarities.
L L
734. With respect to counsel, in considering the appropriate orders to make by way
M M
of disciplinary action, I have not been assisted by being referred to the multiple Statements of
Disciplinary Action promulgated by the Commission nor to several Determinations of this
N N
Tribunal. Many of those decisions involved trading in undisclosed securities accounts by
O employees of securities firm. Profits were made. Trading was concealed. Sometimes false O
statements and representations were made by the miscreant employee in aid of the concealment.
P P
Sometimes the conduct was determined to be dishonest, or on other occasions deliberate and
dishonest. Different lengths of prohibitions from re-entering the industry were ordered.
Q Q
735. The factual circumstances of those cases are wholly different from those
R R
obtaining in the case of Mr. Choi.
S S
T T
U U
- 195 -
V V

A A
Conclusion
B B
736. In all the circumstances, I am satisfied that the Commission’s determination that
a period of prohibition for 2 years from the activities articulated in its Decision Notice is
C C
entirely appropriate.
D D
Part 12 - Orders
E E
737. For the reasons set out above, I reject the submissions made on behalf of Mr.
F F
Choi that the following acts of the Commission were ultra vires its statutory powers, namely
that:
G G
(i) the Investigation commenced by the Commission, pursuant to section 194(1)(a)
H H
and (b) of the Ordinance;
I (ii) the Notice, dated 17 October 2017, issued pursuant to section 183(1) of the I
Ordinance, requiring Mr. Choi to attend an interview;
J J
(iii) the NPDA, issued pursuant to sections 194 and 196 of the Ordinance, dated
16 December 2020; and
K K
(iv) the Decision Notice, issued pursuant to sections 194 and 196 of the Ordinance,
L L
dated 14 January 2022.
M M
738. In those circumstances, I decline to grant the related relief sought on behalf of
N Mr. Choi. N
O O
739. Having conducted a review de novo I have made various determinations, set out
in detail earlier, in respect of Mr. Choi’s conduct in both Project Oasis and Project Frontier. In
P P
summary, I found that Mr. Choi conducted himself on different occasions in breach of some or

4.1 of the CFA Code of Conduct.
R R

S S
740. In light of the determinations made earlier, and having had regard to the
submissions of the parties, I re-affirm the order that the Commission made in the Decision
T T
Notice by way of sanctions, pursuant to sections 194 and 196 of the Ordinance, namely that Mr.
U U

(b) applying to be approved under section 126(1) of the Ordinance, as a responsible

officer of a licensed corporation;
E (c) applying to be given consent to act as an executive officer of a registered E

(d) seeking through a registered institution to have his name entered in the register

Ordinance as that of a person engaged by the registered institution in respect of

certificate for three counsel, to be taxed if not agreed. 703
