title: SFAT Application No. 4/2022 (Ruling)
text:
Application No. 4 of 2022
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under sections
194 and 196 of the Securities and Futures
Ordinance, Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CHOI CHI KIN, CALVIN Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr. Michael Lunn, GBS, Chairman
Date of Ruling: 29 September 2023
____________________
RULING
_________________________
1. In a letter to the Tribunal, dated 25 April 2023, Mr. Choi’s solicitors,
Jingtian & Gongcheng, invited the Tribunal to place no weight on a Confidentiality
Agreement between Xinte Energy Co., Ltd (“Xinte”), UBS AG (“UBS”) and GF Capital
(Hong Kong) (“GF Capital”) dated 24 August 2014, which had been provided to the
Tribunal by Xinte in a letter dated 14 April 2023. Complaint was made that Mr. Choi’s

A A
legal advisers did not have the opportunity to review the Confidentiality Agreement before
B it was produced by Xinte. B
C 2. It was contended that the Confidentiality Agreement “…forms no part of C
the SFC’s case, whether in the Notice of Proposed Disciplinary Action, or the Decision
D D
Notice, or indeed in the SFC’s submissions.” It was stated that if the Tribunal was inclined
“to place any weight, reference or reliance” on the Confidentiality Agreement, it was
E E
requested that Mr. Choi be afforded “…a reasonable opportunity to address the Tribunal
F on any suggested line of relevance or reliance, including by making written and/or oral F
submissions and/or submitting further evidence to the Tribunal in respect of the
G G
Confidentiality Agreement.”
H H
Background
I I
3. The Confidentiality Agreement had been provided by Xinte in its letter,
dated 14 April 2023, in response to the Notice of the Tribunal, dated 6 April 2023, pursuant
J J
to section 219(1)(b) of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571.
K K
4. Following the conclusion of the oral hearings on 16 December 2022, it
L became apparent to the Tribunal that the Confidentiality Agreement, dated 24 August 2014, L
to which reference was made specifically in the Engagement Letter, dated 19 March 2015,
M M
between Xinte Energy, UBS and GF Capital, which was included in the Hearing Bundles,
was not included. By a letter to the parties, dated 2 March 2023, the Tribunal invited the
N N
parties to indicate where, in the material served on the Tribunal, the document could be
O located. O
P 5. Throughout, the Tribunal provided copies to the Commission and Jingtian P
& Gongcheng, of all the correspondence, service of Notices and the replies of the recipients
Q Q
the Tribunal received.
R R
6. By a letter to the Tribunal, dated 3 March 2023, the Commission indicated
that the Confidentiality Agreement was not in the material provided to the Tribunal nor in
S S
the possession of the Commission. The Commission offered to approach UBS to obtain a
T copy of the document. By a letter to the Tribunal, dated 3 March 2023, Jingtian & T
Gongcheng, informed the Tribunal that the document was not in the Applicant’s posession
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
and acknowledged having received the letters sent between the Commission and the
B Tribunal that day. B
C Notices pursuant to section 219 (b) of the Ordinance C
D D
(i) UBS
E 7. In a letter to the Commission, dated 3 March 2023, the Tribunal thanked the E
Commission for its offer of assistance and asked that a Notice to UBS to produce the
F F
document, pursuant to section 219 (b) of the Securities and Futures Ordinance (the
“Ordinance”) , be drafted for the Chairman’s signature. The Tribunal issued a Notice
G G
signed by the Chairman, dated 7 March 2023 directed to UBS to produce the document and
H invited the Commission to serve the Notice on UBS. In a letter to the Tribunal, dated 31 H
March 2023, UBS AG informed the Tribunal that it was unable to locate a copy of the
I document. I
J J
(ii) GF Capital and Xinte
K 8. In a letter to the Commission, dated 4 April 2023, the Tribunal asked for the K
assistance of the Commission to draft Notices to produce the document directed at each of
L L
GF Capital (Hong Kong) Limited and Xinte Energy Co., Ltd. The Tribunal issued Notices
to each of those parties signed by the Chairman, dated 6 April 2023, directing each of them
M M
to produce the document and invited the Commission to serve the Notice on them.
N N
9. In a letter to the Tribunal, dated 14 April 2023, Xinte produced to the
O Tribunal a copy of the Confidentiality Agreement, dated 24 August 2014, pointing out that O
it had been entered into by Xinte’s controlling shareholder, TEBA Co., Ltd. The agreement
P P
was in Chinese characters. By an email, dated 19 April 2023, GF Capital (Hong Kong)
Limited informed the Tribunal that they were unable to locate a copy of the Confidentiality
Q Q
Agreement.

10. In a letter, dated 18 April 2023 the Tribunal provided the parties with a copy
S of the Confidentiality Agreement and invited the Commission to provide an English S
translation of the document.
T T
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
11. In a letter from the Commission, dated 26 April 2023, an English translation
B of the Confidentiality Agreement was provided to the Tribunal. B
C Directions C
D D
12. In response to the request made in the letter of Jingtian & Gongcheng, dated
25 April 2023, that Mr. Choi be afforded a reasonable opportunity to address the Tribunal,
E E
in a letter, dated 27 April 2023, the Tribunal replied:
F F
“Subject to any submissions to the contrary by the respondent, the Chairman
is minded to accede to that request and invites you to consult the respondent
G
to arrive at an estimate of the likely time required for an oral hearing and to
G
provide that estimate to the Tribunal, together with any suggested timetable

H H
I I
13. By a letter to the Tribunal, dated 28 April 2023, the Commission set out its
position:
J J
“We have no objection to the Request in that the Applicant be given a
K K
reasonable opportunity to address the Tribunal regarding any suggested line
of relevance or reliance. If the Applicant applies to adduce further evidence,
L the Respondent will address any such application when made.” L

14. No response having been received by the Tribunal to its letter to Jingtian &
Gongcheng, dated 25 April 2023, by a letter, dated 9 June 2023, the Tribunal issued
N N
Directions to the Applicant, namely that:
O O
“… if the applicant wishes to make any further submissions to the Tribunal
P on that issue the applicant is to file such submissions with the Tribunal on P
or before 5 pm on 15 June 2023.”
Q Q
The Applicant’s submissions
R R
Relevance
S S
15. In written Supplemental Submissions, dated 15 June 2023, Mr. Shieh
T T
submitted that the Confidentiality Agreement “…is wholly irrelevant to the determination
of this Application, because it forms no part of the SFC’s charges against Mr. Choi, and
U U
- 4 -
V V

A A
the SFC does not and cannot now rely on it in an opposition to the Application.” Mr. Shieh
B pointed out that the Confidentiality Agreement was not in the evidence adduced by the B
Commission in the review and formed no part of the Commission’s case in the NPDA,
C C
Decision Notice or submissions made by the Commission to the Tribunal.
D D
Jurisdiction
E E
16. In addition to that primary submission, Mr. Shieh submitted that the
Tribunal did not have jurisdiction to rely on the Confidentiality Agreement. To do so,
F F
would be to “commence a new general enquiry.” The Tribunal did not have the power,
“…to broaden the matters into which it is obliged to enquire”1. That was impermissible.
G G
H Prejudice H
17. Mr. Shieh submitted that reliance on the Confidentiality Agreement at this
I I
stage would cause prejudice to Mr. Choi. He was entitled to have been informed of the
J charges and the allegations against him in good time, so that he could take advice and J
consider what evidence to call.
K K
18. Notwithstanding the fact that no application had been made to the Tribunal
L L
for leave to file any evidence, attached to the Supplemental Submissions was a witness
statement of Madam Guo Junxiang, dated 13 June 2023. For his part, Mr. Shieh informed
M M
the Tribunal that, if the Tribunal was “inclined to place any weight, reference or reliance”
N on the Confidentiality Agreement, Mr. Choi “…seeks leave to file and to rely on the N
attached witness statement of Guo Junxiang in respect of the Confidentiality Agreement”.
O O
The Commission’s submissions
P P
19. In written Supplemental Submissions, dated 26 June 2023, Mr. Li opposed
Q Q
the application to adduce the witness statement of Madam Guo “…in the strongest terms”.
He described it as being, “…nothing but a thinly disguised, belated and desperate ploy to
R R
reopen wholesale his case on Project Oasis.”
S S
T T
1 Moody's Investors Service Hong Kong Limited v Securities and Futures Commission - SFAT 4/2014
U (31 March 2016) at paragraphs 121 and 154. U
- 5 -
V V

A A
20. Of the Commission’s position on the Confidentiality Agreement, Mr. Li
B said that it was straightforward: B
C “The SFC has not previously relied on it, does not need to rely on it, and C
also does not propose to rely on it either.”
D D
21. Perhaps, fearful that there might be a doubt as to the position taken by the
E E
Commission, described as straightforward and articulated above, throughout the written
Supplemental Submissions Mr. Li felt it necessary to re-assert the position taken by the
F F
Commission as to the Confidentiality Agreement, namely that: (i) it does not rely on it;2
G (ii) it does not need to rely on it3; (iii) it does not propose to rely on it.4 G
H H
22. Separately, Mr. Li said of the Confidentiality Agreement that the
Commission, “…does not invite the Tribunal to rely on it either.” He went on to submit,
I I
“…the Tribunal does not need to rely on the Confidentiality Agreement to find against
Choi.”
J J
K Relevance K
23. Of the relevance of the Confidentiality Agreement, Mr. Li said that the
L L
Commission, “…does not accept Choi’s submissions that the Confidentiality Agreement
M is altogether “irrelevant” to the matters which the Tribunal has to decide.” Given that it had M
been, “… expressly incorporated into the Engagement Letter between UBS and Xinte, the
N N
confidentiality obligations it imposed on UBS would be pertinent to the relationship
between UBS and Xinte.”
O O
Jurisdiction
P P
24. Mr. Li invited the Tribunal to reject Mr. Shieh’s submission that having
Q Q
regard to the Confidentiality Agreement was outwith the Tribunal’s remit to consider
R evidence that was not referred to in the NPDA and Decision Notice. That was too narrow R
approach. In an hearing de novo the Tribunal had power to receive fresh evidence. The
S
Tribunal would not be embarking on a “new general enquiry”, if it was to consider the
S
T T
2 The Commission's Supplemental Submissions: paragraph 4; paragraph 15; paragraph 21; and paragraph 44.
3 The Commission's Supplemental Submissions: paragraph 5; paragraph 20.
U 4 The Commission's Supplemental Submissions: paragraph 20; paragraph 30. U
- 6 -
V V

A A
Confidentiality Agreement in its determination. The issue of the conflict between Mr.
B Choi’s role as Xinte’s advisor and his assistance to LR Capital was always the B
Commission’s case. The Confidentiality Agreement was merely another piece of evidence
C C
relevant to that issue.
D D
Prejudice
E E
25. Mr. Li submitted that there was no merit in the complaint that Mr. Choi
would suffer prejudice if reliance was placed on the Confidentiality Agreement. The
F F
confidential nature of the term sheet and the draft SPA was never disputed. The existence
of the Confidentiality Agreement was in evidence. The only new development was that the
G G
provision of the document established the terms of the Confidentiality Agreement .
H H
The Tribunal’s own initiative
I I
26. Mr. Li submitted that it lay within the Tribunal’s jurisdiction and remit on
J J
its own initiative to “consider, refer to, and/or rely on the existence and contents of the
Confidential Agreement in its determination.” If the Tribunal did so, that would not afford
K K
a basis for Mr. Choi to adduce Madam Guo Junxiang’s witness statement into evidence.
Only one part of the witness statement dealt with the uncontroversial fact that the parties
L L
had executed the Confidentiality Agreement of 24 August 2014. The rest of the statement
M was, “ …in reality an application to adduce fresh post-hearing evidence which has nothing M
to do with the contents of the Confidentiality Agreement.”
N N
The Applicant’s Reply Submissions
O O
27. In the Applicant’s Reply Submissions, dated 28 June 2023, it was submitted
P P
that, given that the Commission did not rely on the Confidentiality Agreement, “…the
Q review jurisdiction of the Tribunal does not permit it to place weight” on that document. Q
R A consideration of the submissions R
S 28. As is readily apparent from the nature of the objections made on behalf Mr. S
Choi to the Tribunal placing “any weight, reference or reliance” on the Confidentiality
T T
Agreement, and having regard to the chronology of events set out earlier, it is clear that
U U
- 7 -
V V

A A
objection could have been taken to the process initiated by the Tribunal at the outset on
B 3 March 2023. That was not done. B
C 29. I am satisfied that the Confidentiality Agreement is relevant to the C
Tribunal’s considerations. It is clearly intimately linked to the Engagement Letter, dated
D D
19 March 2015. The fact that the Confidentiality Agreement was not amongst the material
sought and obtained in the responses by UBS to the multiple Notices served on UBS
E E
appears to have been overlooked by the Commission. The Engagement Letter, dated 19
F March 2015, was provided to the Commission by UBS in a response, dated 3 August 2018.5 F
That, was in response to a specific request for that document in a Notice, dated 24 July
G G
2018. The obvious relevance of the Confidentiality Agreement to the confidentiality clause
in the Engagement Letter was readily apparent. Of course, UBS was a party to both the
H H
Confidentiality Agreement and the Engagement Letter.
I I
30. I am satisfied that the Tribunal has jurisdiction to receive and consider the
J document and that the Tribunal has power to do so, and to do so on its own initiative. J
K 31. Nevertheless, having regard to the strident objections made in the K
submissions on behalf of Mr. Choi, together with the repeated statements made in
L L
submissions on behalf of the Commission that the Commission has not relied on and does
not propose to rely on the Confidentiality Agreement, in conjunction with the
M M
Commission’s specific submission that it does not invite the Tribunal to rely on the
N Confidentiality Agreement, in my judgement it would not be appropriate for the Tribunal N
to “receive and consider” the Confidentiality Agreement, as provided by sections 219(1)(a)
O
of the Ordinance. For the avoidance of any doubt, given that the Tribunal has been provided
O
with a copy of the Confidentiality Agreement by Xinte, the Tribunal will not give any
P P
weight to or place any reliance on that document at all.
Q Q
32. In light of that determination, the contingent application to adduce the
R witness statement of Madam Guo Junxiang falls away. R
S S
T T
5 Bundle 2, page 626 at A. 1) and Bundle 3; pages 1191-1209, at page 1199 clause 2 (b).
U U
- 8 -
V V

title: SFAT Application No. 1/2021
text:
Application No. 1 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF the Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CHRISTOPHER JAMES AARONS Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Professor Chan Ka-lok, Member
Mr Vincent Chan Chun-hung, Member
Date of Hearing: 7, 8, 12-14 April & 10 May 2022
Date of Determination: 27 September 2022
____________________
DETERMINATION
________________________

A A
Introduction
B B
1. At all material times, the Applicant in this matter, Christopher
C C
James Aarons, was the Chief Executive Officer of Trafalgar Capital Management
D (HK) Ltd (‘Trafalgar’), a corporation licensed to conduct regulated activities in the D
field of asset management under the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap 571
E E
(‘the Ordinance’).
F F
2. Pursuant to the terms of the Ordinance, the Applicant was
G accredited to Trafalgar as a licensed representative and was approved to act as its G
Responsible Officer. He was therefore a ‘regulated person’ pursuant to the
H H
provisions of s.194(7) of the Ordinance.
I I
3. As part of its business, Trafalgar managed an investment fund, the
J J
Trafalgar Trading Fund (‘the Trafalgar Fund’), which, among other geographical
locations, conducted investment activities in South Korea (‘Korea’). At all material
K K
times, the Applicant was responsible for overseeing the investment strategies of the
L
Trafalgar Fund.
L
M M
4. In terms of a notice dated 13 February 2020, the Securities and
N Futures Commission (‘the SFC’) informed the Applicant that it was considering N
taking disciplinary action against him on the basis that he was not a “fit and proper
O O
person” to continue to be licensed as a representative or to remain approved as a
responsible officer of Trafalgar.
P P
Q Q
5. The disciplinary action contemplated by the SFC was founded on a
provisional view that the Applicant had failed to comply with the ‘Code of Conduct
R R
for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and Futures Commission’
S (‘the Code of Conduct’), more specifically that he had failed to live up to the S
requirements of General Principle 1 of the Code of Conduct, namely, a failure to
T T
conduct his business affairs fairly and in the best interests of the integrity of the
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
market, and also General Principle 7, namely, a failure to comply with all regulatory
B requirements of his business so as to promote the integrity of the market. B
C C
6. As to the issue of sanction, the SFC was provisionally of the view
D that the Applicant’s failure to live up to the requirements of the Code of Conduct D
constituted a threat of sufficient seriousness to the integrity of the market that -
E E
pursuant to s.194(1) of the Ordinance – it warranted the suspension of both the
Applicant’s licence to act as a representative and his approval to act as a responsible
F F
officer for a period of three years.
G G
7. The SFC’s issue of the notice of proposed disciplinary action was
H H
fundamentally influenced by, if not founded on, proceedings that had earlier taken
I place in Korea. Those proceedings had commenced with investigations carried out I
by two Korean regulatory authorities: the Korean Securities and Futures
J J
Commission (‘the KSFC’) and the Korean Financial Supervisory Services (‘the
FSS’). After investigation, those two authorities had concluded that the Applicant
K K
had breached the provisions of the Korean Financial Investment and Capital
L
Markets Act by dealing in the shares of a securities company listed on the Korean
L
Exchange, that company being Hyundai Securities Co., Ltd. (‘Hyundai’) and doing
M M
so based on material non-public information in circumstances that prohibited such
N dealing. The Applicant had been fined a sum of KRW377.6 million, this being the N
equivalent of the profits calculated to have been generated by the impugned
O O
transactions.
P P
8. The Applicant had appealed those regulatory determinations to the
Q Q
Seoul Administrative Court. This judicial body, however, in a reasoned judgment,
had dismissed the appeal, confirming the monetary penalty1. The Applicant, who
R R
had been legally represented in Korea, had chosen not to seek any further appeal
S S
1 In its judgment, the Seoul Administrative Court had spoken of the offence of acting on ‘material non-
T T
public information’ contrary to Article 178-2(1)1.B.(2) of the Financial Investment Services and Capital
Markets Act.
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
and had paid his fine. The judgment of the Seoul Administrative Court had therefore
B constituted the final and conclusive determination in that jurisdiction. B
C C
9. In its Notice of Proposed Disciplinary Action of 13 February 2020,
D the SFC informed the Applicant that – D
E E
‘On the basis of the information before us, the SFC is of the
preliminary view that you are not a fit and proper person to remain
F licensed, in that the Seoul Administrative Court found that you F
dealt in the shares of Hyundai based on material non-public
information, in breach of Articles 178-2(1) and 429-2 of the Korean
G G
Financial Investment Services and Capital Markets Act.
H
Your contravention of the Capital Markets Act suggests that you
H
have breached General Principles 1 (honesty and fairness) and 7
I (compliance) of the Code of Conduct.’ I
J J
10. In his response to the Notice of Proposed Disciplinary Action
issued by the SFC, the Applicant denied being culpable of any form of conduct in
K K
respect of his dealings in Hyundai shares that offended the Code of Conduct. It was
L his assertion that the proceedings in Korea which had found him culpable of market L
misconduct had been vitiated by procedural unfairness and had, in any event, been
M M
wrong in their findings.
N N
11. The Applicant’s submissions were not accepted. In its Decision
O O
Notice of the 29 January 2021, the SFC confirmed its provisional findings both as
to culpability and sanction.
P P
Q
12. The Applicant has now come before this Tribunal seeking a “full
Q
merits review (on both culpability and sanction)”2 against the disciplinary decision
R R
of the SFC.
S S
T T
2 This adopts the wording of the application for review.
U U
- 4 -
V V

A A
An initial overview
B B
13. As to the activities in respect of which the SFC found the Applicant
C C
to be culpable, they concerned a block trade of Hyundai shares which took place on
D 7 January 2016. The shares that made up the block trade exceeded 22.5 million in D
number.
E E
14. Credit Suisse (Hong Kong) Limited (‘Credit Suisse’) was one of
F F
the underwriters of the block trade. Credit Suisse employed a man by the name of
G Kelvin Leung on its marketing team for this particular block trade and it was Kelvin G
Leung who, on 6 January 2016, at about 5.30 in the evening, telephoned the
H H
Applicant at his office to seek his participation as a buyer in the block trade of the
I shares scheduled for the following day. I
J J
15. Block trades can be described as large, privately negotiated
securities transactions. Block trades are arranged in private to circumvent the almost
K K
inevitable downward pressure on the price of the securities when such large numbers
L
are traded on the open market. Block trades are often promoted by investment banks
L
(such as Credit Suisse). Depending on their size, block trades may be broken down
M M
into a number of orders with institutional investors, those investors invariably
N purchasing the shares at a discount. N
O O
16. Before a block trade is made generally known, identifying
knowledge of that block trade - by reason of its ability to materially influence the
P P
market - is invariably considered to constitute material non-public information.
Q Q
17. In the context of this application for review, it can be said that
R R
material non-public information is information concerning a company – in the
S present case, Hyundai – that has not yet been made public but, if made known S
generally to the market, would likely have a material impact on its share price. It is
T T
U U
- 5 -
V V

A A
therefore - considered in principle, but not necessarily in each of its constituent
B elements3 - the equivalent of what in Hong Kong is called ‘inside information’. B
C C
18. Importantly for the purposes of this review, such information would
D include information that does not specifically identify the company but which D
allows for such identification and which therefore gives an advantage to an investor
E E
in possession of that information.
F F
19. That being the case, persons such as Kelvin Leung of Credit Suisse
G (sell-side brokers) who are seeking the participation of investors such as the G
Applicant (buy-side participants) in a block trade, and offering to impart to them
H H
information that will enable them to identify the shares making up the block trade,
I will invariably require those potential investors to agree to be ‘wall-crossed’, that is, I
to agree to keep the information they are about to receive confidential until the block
J J
trade has been publicly announced.
K K
20. To be wall-crossed therefore in respect of an intended block trade
L
is to be formally committed to confidentiality and – importantly - that includes a
L
commitment to refrain from dealing in any way in the shares which are the subject
M M
of the block trade until that trade has been made known to the market.
N N
21. It follows, of course, that the initial responsibility of ensuring that
O O
a potential participant in a block trade is wall-crossed at the appropriate time, that
is, invariably, before identifying information is imparted, lies on the shoulders of
P P
the sell-side broker.
Q Q
R R
S 3 In this respect, for example, it was submitted by the Applicant’s leading counsel, Mr. Peter Duncan SC, S
that there appeared to be no requirement of knowledge under the Korean legislation, that is, that, to be
found culpable, it was not necessary to know at the time of dealing that you had material non-public
T T
information in your possession. For reasons which appear later in this determination, the Tribunal has not
found it necessary to resolve this issue (which is essentially one of Korean law).
U U
- 6 -
V V

A A
22. In this regard, during the course of the hearing, the Tribunal was
B informed that best practice often was to ensure that sell-side brokers adhere to pre- B
prepared wall-crossing scripts. In the present case, when Kelvin Leung telephoned
C C
the Applicant on the evening of 6 January 2016 to canvas the Applicant’s interest in
D the block trade, there is no evidence of the use by him of any pre-prepared wall- D
crossing script4.
E E
23. What must be emphasised, however, is that even if Kelvin Leung
F F
himself fell below acceptable standards in the discharge of his duties that evening,
G the obligation to maintain the strictures of confidentiality when material non-public G
information is passed is a mutual obligation, one shared by the sell-side broker and
H H
the buy-side participant.
I I
24. It is no surprise of course that, for a host of reasons, best practice
J J
may not always be followed. In this regard, by way of illustration, Ms. Yak Chau
Wei, the expert witness called by the SFC, testified that the United Kingdom
K K
Financial Conduct Authority in 2015 had published a paper titled “Asset
L
Management Firms and the Risk of Market Abuse” which spoke of there being a
L
lack of effective policies in many asset management firms to identify unintentional
M M
inside information being received in the course of the investment process.
N N
25. It was also said in the report of the Hong Kong Market Misconduct
O O
Tribunal into the dealings in the shares of Chaoda Modern Agriculture (Holdings)
Limited5, that, notwithstanding the circumstances in which inside information is
P P
imparted during calls sounding out financial investments, if inside information is
Q Q
received, the party receiving it is constrained from dealing on the basis of that
information.
R R
4 As it is, Kelvin Leung played no part in the hearing before the Tribunal; he did not give evidence nor was
S S
any statement by him admitted into evidence. There was, however, evidence of him sending the Applicant
a written confirmation of wall-crossing after the Applicant had been given full details of the intended
block trade and that confirmation is referred to later in this determination.
T T
5 Dated 20 April 2012.
U U
- 7 -
V V

A A
B 26. Accordingly, if material non-public information is passed in the B
course of a conversation of the kind initiated by Kelvin Leung on the evening of
C C
6 January 2016, even if the sell-side broker neglects to formally read out a wall-
D crossing script or to seek consent to be wall-crossed from the buy-side participant, D
that of itself does not free the buy-side participant from the obligations of
E E
confidentiality. During the course of such conversations, a party being approached
– a buy-side participant - has the obligation at all times to consider the nature of the
F F
material being imparted and whether the nature and extent of that information binds
G him or her to confidentiality. Put simply, a buy-side participant cannot take G
advantage of the delay or negligence of a sell-side broker by acting for purposes of
H H
gain on what he knows to be material non-public information received. Such
I conversations embody mutual good faith and adherence to fair conduct. If it was I
otherwise, the essential integrity of these important dealings would be undermined.
J J
27. At this juncture, it should be observed that at no time did the
K K
Applicant dispute the principles just explained and outlined.
L L
28. In the present case, it is agreed that, when Kelvin Leung telephoned
M M
the Applicant on the evening of 6 January 2016 to inform him of the intended block
N trade of equities and to canvas his interest in participating in that sale, he did not at N
any time state the name of the shares that were the subject of the block sale, that is,
O O
that they were Hyundai shares. However, in the course of that conversation, it is in
no way disputed – all such conversations being audio recorded - that Kelvin Leung
P P
gave the following information to the Applicant –
Q Q
(a) that there was to be a secondary sell down of shares [a form of block
R R
trade] in a Korean securities company, that sell down being
S scheduled for the following day, that is, within about the next 24 S
hours;
T T
U U
- 8 -
V V

A A
(b) that, by way of incentive to participants, the secondary sell down
B [the block trade] was expected to be subject to a high single digit B
discount or higher;
C C
(c) that the block trade itself would have a value of approximately
D D
US$100 million;
E E
(d) that, in respect of the securities company itself, it had a market
capitalisation of ‘over’ US$1 billion; and
F F
(e) it also had daily transactions – a daily gross revenue – ‘close’ to
G G
US$5 million.
H H
I 29. It has further been agreed that, very shortly after the Applicant had I
been given this information – indeed, within seconds of being given it - he informed
J J
Kelvin Leung that he would have to briefly interrupt their telephone conversation
K as he had an urgent piece of business to complete. He said that he would telephone K
back “in a moment” to complete the conversation. The break in the conversation
L L
was therefore – on the basis of what was said by the Applicant – intended to be no
more than a few minutes.
M M
N 30. As it transpires, however, the Applicant failed to make his promised N
return call, either that evening or the following morning. Indeed, the Applicant and
O O
Kelvin Leung did not speak to each other again until late morning the following
P morning and it was then Kelvin Leung who made the call in order to complete his P
exchange with the Applicant: including wall-crossing him.
Q Q
31. As to why the Applicant did not make his return call, it was his case
R R
that he simply forgot to do so. He became occupied with other matters. What then
S
was the nature of those other matters?
S
T T
U U
- 9 -
V V

A A
32. It was the Applicant’s unchallenged evidence that, when he
B interrupted his telephone conversation with Kelvin Leung, he immediately dealt B
with the time-sensitive business that was at hand. In this regard, the Applicant
C C
placed papers into evidence showing that he had that evening been involved in a
D transaction relating to Thailand6 . D
E E
33. That piece of business, however, only took a few minutes. What
then happened? The Applicant said that, as he was interested in participating in the
F F
block trade but was aware of the volatility of the Korean market, he decided to give
G consideration to relevant data available to him related to the ‘securities company’ G
sector of the Korean market: he described it as ‘eye-balling’ the data available to
H H
him on his computer terminal.
I I
34. It was, however, the Applicant’s case that, notwithstanding what he
J J
had been told by Kelvin Leung, at no stage in his consideration of that data did he
believe himself to be to be in possession of what constituted – or could well
K K
constitute – material non-public information.
L L
35. The Applicant said that, while he could not be certain that the block
M M
trade would go ahead as predicted by Kelvin Leung, he thought it likely that it would
N go ahead and, if so, it also likely that he would participate. However, as a counter to N
the unpredictability at that time of the Korean market, he needed an effective hedge.
O O
His concern, therefore, was to seek out that effective hedge and, in doing so, he was
principally concerned with issues of liquidity. His whole mindset, therefore, was
P P
focused on matters relevant to hedging, it was not focused on the issue of identifying
Q Q
the company that was the subject of the intended block trade.
R R
S S
6 It is to be noted that these materials had not been placed before the Korean authorities. In light of that
lacuna, as counsel for the SFC put it, it was not open to the Applicant to criticise the findings of the
T T
Korean investigative authorities and the court that he had terminated the conversation with Kelvin Leung
on a ‘pretext’.
U U
- 10 -
V V

A A
36. As it was, after he had considered the data available to him, the
B Applicant executed short swaps in the shares of two Korean securities companies. B
The first company, the one that was central to the Applicant’s strategy, was Hyundai
C C
itself, the company that was the subject of the intended block trade. The second
D company, which played a far lesser role in the Applicant’s strategy, was Kiwoom D
Securities Co., Ltd (‘Kiwoom’).
E E
37. It was the Applicant’s case that, once he had identified these two
F F
companies, he took immediate steps to execute his hedging strategy.
G G
38. It was implicit in his evidence that he did not think first to make his
H H
promised telephone call to Kelvin Leung. He had forgotten that obligation. Of
I course, as the Applicant himself accepted, if he had remembered and made his return I
call, and if he had then been formally wall-crossed by Kelvin Leung - or had been
J J
given additional information which constituted material non-public information - he
would then that same evening have been prevented from dealing in any way with
K K
the shares of Hyundai, even for purposes of hedging.
L L
39. What then, in outline, were the Applicant’s dealings? The
M M
Applicant’s dealings consisted of entering into a number of short swaps. As the
N Tribunal understands it, short swaps may be employed equally as a hedge or as a N
mechanism for short selling.
O O
40. By way of a broad conceptual understanding, it can be said that
P P
short selling is a bet by a dealer that the price of an equity will fall and, if that event
Q Q
occurs, a profit is made. It has been the SFC contention that, knowing that the block
trade would inevitably lead to a decline in the value of the shares in the market once
R R
knowledge of it was generally known, and discovering – by way of the information
S imparted to him by Kelvin Leung – that the subject of the block trade was Hyundai, S
the Applicant abused that information by shorting Hyundai shares.
T T
U U
- 11 -
V V

A A
41. Contrary to this assertion, it is the Applicant’s case that, as he has
B consistently maintained, at the time of entering into the short swaps he did not know B
the identity of the company which was to be the subject of the block trade. His
C C
actions were in no way impermissible but, to the contrary, were, on a correct
D understanding, taken as a recognised means of hedging the position of the Trafalgar D
Fund without in any way relying on what in Hong Kong would be termed ‘inside
E E
information’.
F F
42. What then were the actions taken by the Applicant? The following
G is a summary (taken largely from the Agreed Statement of Facts filed by the parties) G
–
H H
(a) At about 5.50pm, some 20 minutes after he had truncated his
I telephone conversation with Kelvin Leung, the Applicant checked I
with BNP Paribas Securities Services (‘BNP Paribas’) on the
J J
available inventory of Hyundai shares and Kiwoom shares for short
swaps.
K K
(b) At around 7.40pm, BNP Paribas confirmed short swap orders for
L L
500,000 shares in Hyundai and just 35,000 shares in Kiwoom.
M M
(c) The order was formally placed at about 7.40am the following
N morning by Andy Scott, a manager of Trafalgar. N
O (d) At 8.02 that same morning, the Applicant checked with Merrill O
Lynch Asia Pacific Limited (‘Merrill Lynch’) as to its inventory of
P P
the two shares for short swaps. Merrill Lynch confirmed that it held
2,000,000 Hyundai and 100,000 Kiwoom.
Q Q
(e) At about 10.36am, on the Applicant’s instructions, Andy Scott
R R
confirmed with Merrill Lynch an order for short swaps of 500,000
S
Hyundai shares.
S
T (f) In the result, the Applicant ordered one million Hyundai shares for T
short swaps.
U U
- 12 -
V V

A A
(g) As to execution, that morning, between 8.00 and 11.00am, 500,000
B Hyundai shares via short swaps were executed through BNP B
Paribas and a further 54,493 Hyundai shares were executed through
C C
Merrill Lynch, this being the first tranche of the one million shares
D due by it. D
E E
43. The Applicant therefore arranged short swaps involving 1,000,000
Hyundai shares and just 35,000 Kiwoom shares, that exercise commencing about
F F
20 - 25 minutes after he had broken off his conversation with Kelvin Leung and
G ending at about 11.00 the following morning. G
H H
44. When the Applicant’s short swap dealings in the shares of Hyundai
I were analysed by the Korean regulatory authorities, it was determined that, even I
though the name of Hyundai was never imparted in the telephone conversation that
J J
took place with Kelvin Leung, there was nevertheless sufficient particularity in the
information that was imparted to enable the Applicant, once he was off the telephone,
K K
to employ data available to him in his office to satisfy himself that the information
L
he had received from Kelvin Leung must relate to Hyundai. It was further the
L
findings of the Korean regulatory authorities that the Applicant had then proceeded
M M
to make a financial gain by overseeing a short-swap exercise, effectively, therefore,
N by shorting Hyundai shares, an exercise which was at no time divulged to Kelvin N
Leung, the sell-side broker. These findings were, on appeal, supported by the Seoul
O O
Administrative Court.
P P
45. When the matter of the Applicant’s actions was considered by the
Q Q
SFC, it was satisfied that it could take into the account the findings of the Korean
regulatory authorities and also the Seoul Administrative Court, giving all necessary
R R
weight to those findings.
S S
46. That said, the SFC did not seek to find the Applicant culpable of a
T T
specific offence under the Ordinance. Instead, it found that the manner in which the
U U
- 13 -
V V

A A
Applicant had used the information imparted to him by Kelvin Leung was “less than
B forthright and contrary to the requirement to act fairly and in the best interests of the B
integrity of the market”7.
C C
D 47. As for the Applicant, it has always been his assertion that his single D
concern at the relevant time was to ensure that, if he did decide to participate in the
E E
block trade, he wanted to ensure that the risk he was undertaking was satisfactorily
hedged. Although he was not actively involved in the Korean securities sector, he
F F
could see that there were seven securities companies listed on the Korean Exchange
G that met the market capitalisation and trading volume criteria imparted to him by G
Kelvin Leung. Those seven companies were: Hyundai, Kiwoom, Meritz Co. Ltd,
H H
(‘Meritz’); Korea Investment Holdings (‘KIH’), Daewoo Securities (‘Daewoo’);
I NH Investment & Securities Co. Ltd. (‘NH’) and Samsung Securities Co. Ltd I
(‘Samsung’).
J J
48. As the Applicant put it, according to his understanding, therefore,
K K
with the limited information available to him at the time, Hyundai was just one of
L
seven companies that might be the subject of the block trade.
L
M M
49. Colin Knight, who was called as an expert witness by the Applicant,
N explained the rationale of short swapping (by way of hedging) in these N
circumstances –
O O
“Once a trader becomes aware of the possibility of a block trade in
P P
which he may participate then it is common practice for the trader
to hedge his anticipated exposure by taking a short position. As a
Q Q
large holding may take several hours, or even weeks, to sell, it is
accepted practice to put on some type of hedge, either in the general

place either of those metrics into a specific historical context. He did not give any
O O
specific date or period of time to qualify his descriptions. They were instead placed
essentially into the present context and, as the descriptions were of collected data,
P P
that must have included the recent past.

A. Market capitalisation

158. The market capitalisation of a company is a standard of

A A
measurement: a metric. As such, it measures the approximate size and value of a
B company by calculating the total market value of its issued shares. By way of a B
simple illustration, a company with 10 million issued shares priced (at the time) at
C C
$100 each will have a market capitalisation of $1 billion. As an everyday standard
D of measurement, there is nothing particularly esoteric about it. During the course of D
the hearing it was never disputed that it is, in the finance industry, a well understood
E E
metric.
F F
159. When the Applicant testified before the Tribunal, he said that
G during his telephone conversation with Kelvin Leung, when Kelvin Leung spoke of G
market capitalisation, it was his impression that Kelvin Leung was trying to set a
H H
‘bar of liquidity’ and accordingly, as he understood it, it didn’t matter whether the
I market capitalisation was “one, five or twenty five”. The real question was: is this I
stock liquid enough for your fund? The Applicant accepted, however, that, whether
J J
or not that was Kelvin Leung’s true intention, was supposition on his part.
K K
160. In the judgment of the Tribunal, whatever else the Applicant may
L
have surmised, as an experienced manager in the financial industry, indeed
L
managing a hedge fund, he would still have understood what market capital value,
M M
as an everyday standard of measurement, was intended to mean. Accordingly, when
N Kelvin Leung informed him that the company had a “market cap [of] over a billion N
dollars”, the Tribunal is satisfied that, whatever the more esoteric undertows that
O O
may, or may not, have been surmised by him, the Applicant would also have
understood that ‘market cap’ was fundamentally a metric of value and size.
P P
Q Q
161. As such, the Tribunal is satisfied that, when a market capitalisation
of over a billion US dollars was mentioned by Kelvin Leung, that would have
R R
indicated, on any ordinary understanding, a value in the region of – but ‘above’ -
S one billion dollars. A market capitalisation below a billion US dollars would not S
qualify but equally a market capitalisation exceeding two or three billion dollars
T T
would not have been intended.

M Looking to an appropriate sanction M
N N
195. In considering appropriate sanctions, it must be emphasized that
they are not designed simply to punish an individual for his or her wrongdoing. They
O O
are not, therefore, to be equated with punishment under the criminal law. In the
P
context of this matter, sanctions are designed as a defensive measure, that is, they
P
are designed to protect the integrity of the market. They are designed to better ensure
Q Q
that the market operates in an honest and fair manner, each of these descriptions
R being essential to the maintenance of public confidence. R
S S
196. Of course, sanctions that are imposed in order to protect the
integrity of the market will inevitably cause hurt to culpable individuals. By way of
T T
illustration, if a person judged to constitute a threat to the market is removed from
U U
- 56 -
V V

A A
his or her position within the market, that will inevitably cause financial hurt and no
B doubt also harm to reputation. But these are the inevitable consequences of ensuring B
that the integrity of the market itself is maintained.
C C
D 197. That said, the Tribunal is aware that any sanction, or set of sanctions, D
imposed must take into account relevant mitigating factors particular to the
E E
individual who has been found culpable.

206. It was the SFC’s view that a deterrent message needed to be sent to

D 207. As to the core of the Applicant’s culpability, the Tribunal agrees D
with the findings of the SFC that it is to be found in the deceitful manner in which
E E
he sought to manipulate matters in respect of his dealings with Kelvin Leung, doing
so because he believed that he could exploit the information imparted to him. The
F F
Tribunal is satisfied that, even if he originally broke off his conversation with Kelvin
G Leung for good faith reasons, once he appreciated the possibility of being able to G
exploit the information that he now had in his possession, he then sought in an
H H
underhand way to seek a financial gain by manipulating matters, principally by
I taking the necessary steps to avoid having to speak to Kelvin Leung, doing so in the I
knowledge that he had not yet been formally wall-crossed.

208. To the understanding of the Tribunal, ‘sounding out’ calls of the
K K
kind initiated by Kelvin Leung on the evening of 6 January 2016 are integral to the
L
efficient running of the financial industry. There are, of course, guidelines issued by
L
financial institutions to try and ensure that such calls do not compromise regulatory
M M
constraints. That said, however, in the swift exchange of information in financial
N matters, unless such calls are to be reduced to the level of cumbersome ‘box-ticking N
exercises’, those who initiate such calls must be armed with a level of discretion as
O O
to how best to approach counter-parties and how best to market whatever financial
instrument it is that they have to sell. That is why, as the Tribunal has noted earlier
P P
in this determination, in the course of such conversations, a party being approached
Q Q
- somebody, therefore, in the position of the Applicant in the present case - has the
obligation to consider the nature and extent of the information being imparted and
R R
to determine whether that information binds him or her to confidentiality.

209. In respect of the Applicant’s actions, it is a cause for real concern
T T
that a man of the Applicant’s seniority and experience, a man more particularly who
U U
- 59 -
V V

had earned his high level of responsibility, should have seen fit, while maintaining

towards a colleague in the profession.

D 210. Throughout the evening of 6 January 2016 and well into the D
morning of the following day, it is evident that the Applicant sought to exploit the
E E
information he had obtained (first direct from Kelvin Leung and then through the
analysis of that information) while remaining at a distance from Kelvin Leung and
F F
not being drawn back into a situation of confidentiality which would then have
G prevented him from using the information in any manner at all, either by way of G
hedging or by way of short selling.
H H
I 211. In the circumstances, bearing in mind the position of high I
responsibility held by the Applicant at the time, and the fact that he abused that
J J
responsibility, the Tribunal is satisfied that a period of suspension is required. The
more difficult question is the length of that suspension.
K K
L
212. The Tribunal has been informed that, once the matter of his
L
misconduct arose, the Applicant took early steps to ensure that internal systems in
M M
his company were fortified. That is all to the good and is to be recommended. It is
N to be remembered, however, that it was not so much a system that was at fault in N
this instance, the fault lay rather in the personal conduct of the Applicant.
O O
213. The Tribunal does, however, take into account that, as matters
P P
turned out, the Applicant’s culpable conduct had very little impact on the market.
Q Q
The threat that his actions actually posed was, therefore, minimal. This it considers
to be a material factor.
R R
S 214. In all the circumstances, the Tribunal has come to the determination S
that a period of suspension of two years is the most appropriate sanction. An order
T T
to that effect is made accordingly.
U U
- 60 -
V V

title: SFAT Application No. 1/2021 (Ruling)
text:
Application No. 1 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under section
194 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap.
571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CHRISTOPHER JAMES AARONS Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Date of Ruling: 14 March 2022
_________________________
RULING
(ON MODE OF HEARING)
_________________________

A A
Introduction
B B
1. The substantive hearing of this matter before myself and two members has
C C
been set down for hearing over six days in April of this year, that is, on 7 and 8 April and
thereafter from 11 to 14 April. A difficulty, however, has arisen in respect of those dates
D D
in that, as matters stand, I will be in Australia and unable to return to Hong Kong over that
period of time to sit as Chairman of the review.
E E
F 2. The parties have agreed that, by reason of earlier rulings made, I am now F
seized of this matter and it is no longer possible simply to ask another Chairman of the
G G
Tribunal to take my place. I should remain as Chairman through until this matter has been
determined.
H H
I 3. How am I to do that? As I see it, there are two options. First, the matter may I
be adjourned to a time later in the year when counsel for the parties are free to appear before
J
the Tribunal. That time, it appears, is in November, a delay of some seven months. Second,
J
the hearing may go ahead in April (on the dates set out above) subject to the condition that,
K K
while all other parties will be physically in attendance, I will conduct the proceedings as
Chairman by way of internet connection, that is, by way of what is now commonly called
L L
‘zoom’.
M M
4. The Applicant has expressed a strong desire for the matter to be adjourned.
N N
He considers it fundamental that this particular case be heard with all parties physically
present – most certainly myself and the two Members – in order to ensure a full, fair and
O O
therefore just hearing.
P P
5. The Respondent, the Securities and Futures Commission (“the SFC”), is
Q desirous of further delays being avoided and has submitted that, along with many other Q
cases being determined in different jurisdictions of the common law world in this time of
R R
pandemic, a full and fair hearing can be secured even though, as Chairman, I will
participate by way of internet connection. In short, the SFC has submitted that the hearing
S S
should continue in April.
T T
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
6. The question to be determined in this ruling is which of the two options is
B to be chosen? B
C C
A brief background
D D
7. In about March 2020, the World Health Organization declared that the
illness caused by the spread of what is now known as Covid-19 constituted a global
E E
pandemic. In seeking to combat the pandemic, many states imposed restrictions – including
F prohibition from entry and quarantine requirements - on international travel. Australia and F
Hong Kong were two such countries.
G G
8. In the second half of 2021, Australia, having softened its entry requirements
H H
for international travellers who had been fully vaccinated and who tested negative for the
I coronavirus, began to permit international travellers to enter the country. As I perceived it I
at the time, this was in accord with a world trend.
J J
9. In October 2021, I travelled with my wife to Sydney in order to spend time
K K
with our son and other family members. It was intended that we would return to Hong
Kong in or about mid-January 2022, more than two months prior to the substantive hearing
L L
of this matter.
M M
10. Regrettably, after our departure, increasingly large numbers of people in
N N
Hong Kong began to fall victim to the ravages of a new strain of the Covid 19 coronavirus.
As part of its strategy to combat the pandemic within its borders, the Hong Kong authorities
O O
prohibited persons from a number of designated countries where the Covid pandemic was
P believed to be particularly grave from entering Hong Kong. One of those countries was P
Australia.
Q Q
11. Initially, the prohibitions against inbound travel were for limited periods of
R R
two to three weeks at a time and on each occasion I ensured that my wife and I were booked
to return to Hong Kong on the first permitted date. On each occasion, upon our anticipated
S S
return, we were required to undergo 14 days quarantine in a designated hotel and on each
T occasion, in this respect, the necessary bookings were made. T
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
12. In late February of this year, after I had booked to return to Hong Kong on
B 4 March, the Hong Kong authorities announced that, in order to better combat the spread B
of Covid within its borders, it was now prohibiting persons from the designated countries,
C C
including Australia, from returning to Hong Kong until after 20 April 2022, the period of
14 days quarantine to remain in place1.
D D
13. Although bookings were immediately secured to fly back to Hong Kong on
E E
21 April, it meant that it was now no longer possible to be physically in Hong Kong over
F the period between 7 and 14 April, that is, over the period of the hearing, and it was on this F
basis that I sought the views of the parties as to the best way forward.
G G
The option advocated by the SFC
H H
14. The SFC has argued that there should be no delay. It has submitted that it is
I I
in the interests of the parties and the integrity of the market to resolve this matter without
J further delay. In this regard, I have taken note of the fact that the impugned events J
themselves occurred in January 2016 – some six years ago - while the decision notice which
K gave rise to these proceedings is dated 29 January 2021. K
L L
15. The application for review is dated 19 February 2021. Accordingly, if the
matter is heard in November of this year, there will be a delay in excess of 20 months
M M
between the filing of the application for review and the substantive hearing.
N N
16. It is a fundamental principle that matters in our courts should proceed to
O O
resolution without undue delay. That principle applies equally to matters before the
Tribunal.
P P
17. There is no suggestion that the parties will not be fully prepared for the
Q Q
hearing in April of this year; the single issue is the wholly unanticipated - and prolonged -
R R
S S
1 It should be said that Hong Kong has permitted persons who wish to return to from ‘prohibited’ countries
to travel first to a non-prohibited country (such as Singapore or Thailand) and spending two weeks there
T before entering Hong Kong. Persons doing so are then required to undertake 14 days quarantine. The cost, T
however, of hotel bookings in these ‘sanitising’ countries is high and, in addition, there is no certainty that
these countries will not themselves be placed on the ‘prohibited list’. For those reasons, I made the decision
U
not to attempt this alternate way home.
U
- 4 -
V V

A A
ban on travel into Hong Kong from Australia and the fact, therefore, that I, as Chairman,
B will not be able to be physically present when the hearing takes place. B
C C
18. It is, however, the SFC case that my physical absence can be countered by
my ability to participate fully in the proceedings as Chairman by way of a now well tested
D D
and successful Internet connection; that is, by way of zoom.
E E
19. In this latter respect, the proposed use of a video link is now a well-honed
F tool used to mitigate the various adverse consequences to litigants of the Covid pandemic. F
I am aware that it has been used in Hong Kong proceedings, that it is regularly used in
G G
international arbitration matters and indeed I have recently conducted proceedings in this
Tribunal by way of the same video link. I am aware, therefore, that it can be successfully
H H
used.
I I
The option advocated by the Applicant
J J
20. It has been advocated on behalf of the Applicant, however, that in the
K K
present case it is particularly important that I should be physically present during the course
of the substantive hearing. It has been asserted that the Applicant’s credibility is a central
L L
issue in the proceedings and that his credibility should be scrutinised in the solemn
M atmosphere of a Tribunal hearing in my personal presence. M
N N
21. It is not a contention which I find to be persuasive.
O O
22. Insofar as the demeanour of the Applicant during the course of his testimony
P may be said to be relevant2, I will be able to watch him give his evidence by way of video P
link and able also to hear what he has to say. I will be able fully and effectively to monitor
Q the testimony in my role as Chairman and to weigh its strengths and weaknesses. As a Q
professional judge, I do not see that I need to be physically present to fulfil that function,
R R
not when I am able by way of a video link to have a full and immediate picture of everything
said and done by the Applicant (and by all other witnesses).
S S
T T
2 And, of course, relying too much on demeanour can itself be dangerous. Juries are commonly given
U
warnings in that regard.
U
- 5 -
V V

A A
23. I would add that my earlier experience of chairing a substantive tribunal
B hearing by way of video link has shown me that my ability to ensure the due solemnity of B
proceedings is not in any way prejudiced by my physical absence.
C C
24. As to the suggestion that a video hearing (on my part at least) would inhibit
D D
the normal process of face to face interaction between the Tribunal members to deal with
matters as they arise, my involvement in the proceedings as Chairman will obviously
E E
include my discussions with the Members before the hearing commences and during
F appropriate intervals during the course of the proceedings. The same obviously is to be said F
for our immediate discussions at the end of the substantive hearing3. In this regard it will
G G
be simple enough to set up a separate confidential video link between myself and the
members which will permit private exchanges. If any matter should arise requiring such
H H
discussion during the course of evidence, it will be simple enough to adjourn proceedings
I to enable such discussion to take place: in exactly the same manner as if I was physically I
present.
J J
25. In any event, it is invariably the case that it is better to allow counsel to deal
K K
with matters of evidence first, being allowed to make their submissions, and then for the
Tribunal to consider such issues. Matters of law, of course, rest with me alone.
L L
M 26. In respect of the authority placed before me - King’s Glory Educational M
Centre Ltd versus Tsang Woon Ming and Others4 - the factual circumstances under
N N
consideration in that judgement by A. Chan J were fundamentally different. In that case it
was proposed that witnesses should give evidence to a court in Hong Kong from outside
O O
of the Territory and therefore beyond the physical jurisdiction of the court.
P P
27. On behalf of the applicant, a number of other submissions were put forward
Q which, with respect, I consider to be makeweights. For example, it has been suggested that Q
the hearing might be undermined by the infection of persons involved in its workings. That,
R R
of course, is purely speculative. Having regard to the persistence of the pandemic, such
S S
3 In this regard, it is to be remembered that I should be out of quarantine and back in circulation in Hong
T
Kong in early May and I will then be able to meet with the Members to discuss all relevant issues on a face
T
to face basis.
4 [2020] HKCFI 891
U U
- 6 -
V V

A A
difficulties could also arise later in the year. Should difficulties arise they can be dealt with
B at the time by way of relevant directions. B
C C
28. As to any difference in time zones, as I have said earlier, I have already
conducted one hearing by way of video link from Australia and did not find that to be
D D
difficult. It is my understanding that I will be the only ‘party’ to the proceedings who will
need to be linked to it by way of video.
E E
F My determination F
G G
29. In all the circumstances, stepping back and viewing this matter in the round,
I am satisfied that a full, fair and just hearing can be secured if I am able to participate by
H H
way of video link. It is therefore my direction that the hearing will proceed, as already
I scheduled, in April of this year. I
J J
K K
L L
M M
Michael Hartmann
N N
(Chairman)
O O
P P
Q Q
R R
S S
T T
U U
- 7 -
V V

title: SFAT Application No. 2/2021
text:
Application No. 2 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF the Decisions made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CARDINALASIA CONSULTING LIMITED 1st Applicant
LEE SHIU LUN EDWARD 2nd Applicant
And
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Dates of Hearing: 7, 8, 10, 11, 17 March & 6 April 2022
Date of Determination: 27 January 2023
____________________
DETERMINATION
________________________

A A
The Applicants
B B
1. This application for review has been brought by two Applicants (‘the
C C
Applicants’). The first is a company registered in Hong Kong, Cardinalasia
Consulting Limited (‘Cardinalasia’). The second is the sole shareholder of that
D D
company, Lee Shiu Lun (‘Edward Lee’), a man of high academic qualification and
E
experience in matters of finance.
E
F F
2. At all times relevant to this matter, that is, between about August
2014 and October 2017, the Applicants, that is, both Cardinalasia and Edward Lee,
G G
were licensed by the Securities and Futures Commission (‘the SFC’) to carry on
H H
Type 4 (advising on securities) and Type 9 (asset management) regulated activities
pursuant to the provisions of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap 571 (‘the
I I
Ordinance’). As licensed persons, both Applicants were subject to the Code of
J Conduct1. J
K K
3. Edward Lee, the Second Applicant, was further approved to act as
the Responsible Officer of Cardinalasia. In respect of all matters relevant to this
L L
application for review, it is accepted that Edward Lee has played a primary and
M decisive role in representing Cardinalasia. M
N N
An introductory overview
O O
4. In early 2014, in Hong Kong, the Second Applicant, Edward Lee,
P struck up a business relationship with a man named Yeung Chun Wai (‘Anthony P
Yeung’), also a man of experience in the financial sector. Anthony Yeung intended
Q Q
to set up and run a number of investment funds and Edward Lee agreed to assist him
in this undertaking.
R R
S S
T T
1 That is: the “Code of Conduct for Persons licensed by or registered with the Securities and Futures
Commission”.
U U
- 2 -

A A
5. Although no documentary evidence to that effect was put before the
B Tribunal, it would appear that Anthony Yeung was ‘the promoter’ of the investment B
funds in the sense that he appears to have been primarily responsible for setting up
C C
the funds.
D D
6. Between about April 2014 and June 2015, five investment funds
E
were set up, each fund investing in small and medium enterprise (‘SME’) stocks.
E
F F
7. The offering memorandum given to potential investors by each fund
made it clear that the investment strategy of each would be aggressive in nature,
G G
seeking returns of 20% per annum. To achieve such returns, potential investors were
H H
informed that each fund would employ leverage mechanisms in order to enhance
returns, the limit being around 300% of each fund’s net asset value. That being the
I I
case, it followed that margin financing would be used2.
J J
8. Potential investors were further informed that, with each fund
K K
concentrating on SME stocks, there would be a limited diversification of investment
which itself “may expose the Fund to losses disproportionate to those incurred by
L L
the market in general”.
M M
9. The five funds that were set up – the ‘Funds’ – were:
N N
O (1) the Taiping Quantum China Opportunities Fund launched in April O
2014 (the ‘Opportunities Fund’);
P P
(2) the Taiping Quantum Strategic Fund launched in May 2014 (the
‘Strategic Fund’);
Q Q
(3) the Taiping Quantum Prosperity Fund launched in July 2014 (the
R R
‘Prosperity Fund’);
S S
2 As to the intention of the Funds to borrow money, potential investors were informed that: “The Fund will
T T
employ leverage including, without limitation, through pledge assets as security for borrowings for
enhancement of return. The limit of the leverage is around 300% of the Fund’s net asset value.”
U U
- 3 -

A A
(4) the Quantum Advantage Fund launched in March 2015 (the
B B
Advantage Fund’) and
(5) the Quantum Enhanced Fund launched in June 2015 (the ‘Enhanced
C C
Fund’).
D D
10. Between about August 2014 and October 2017, Cardinalasia, under
E the control of Edward Lee, acted as Principal Investment Adviser (‘Investment E
Adviser’) to these five Funds, each appointment being in terms of an investment
F F
advisory agreement.
G G
11. In December 2020, after a period of investigation, the Securities and
H
Futures Commission (‘the SFC’) informed the two Applicants that it had come to
H
the preliminary determination that, during the time that Cardinalasia, under the
I I
control of Edward Lee, had acted as Investment Adviser to the Funds, the company
J and Edward Lee, as the company’s Responsible Officer, had failed, as licensed J
persons under the Ordinance, to give appropriate advice to the Manager of each
K K
Fund in an attempt to ensure fair dealing in respect of two matters that were at the
time of importance to the financial integrity of the Funds.
L L
M 12. Those matters were, first, a series of loan arrangements entered into M
between the funds at a time when certain of the Funds were experiencing severe
N N
liquidity problems and, second, a little later in time, a series of cross trades entered
O into between the Funds at a time when the Managers of the Funds were seeking a O
rebalancing of their investment portfolios.
P P
13. It was the SFC assertion that the Applicants, in their role as
Q Q
Investment Advisers, had failed to seek to protect the best interests of the Funds in
R respect of the internal loan arrangements and the cross trades and that, in the wake R
of the Applicants’ asserted neglect, certain of the Funds had been prejudiced
S S
financially.
T T
U U
- 4 -

A A
14. In light of these asserted failures, it was the SFC’s preliminary
B finding that the Applicants, as licensed persons, had acted in a culpable manner B
under the Code of Conduct in that they had failed to -
C C
D (a) ensure that the ‘internal loan arrangements’ entered into between the D
Funds had been fair to, and in the best interests of, both the
E E
borrowing and lending Funds, and
F (b) had further failed to properly assess the basis for certain of the cross F
trades conducted between the funds in order to ensure that they had
G G
been executed on the best available terms for, and in the best interests
of, both the buying and selling funds.
H H
I
15. It was further said that Edward Lee, as Responsible Officer for
I
Cardinalasia, in failing to ensure the maintenance of appropriate standards of
J J
conduct, that is, conduct seeking to bring about fair outcomes for investors in the
K funds, had been in breach of General Principle 9 of the Code of Conduct which K
relates to the responsibilities of senior management. General Principle 9 commences
L L
by stating that -
M M
“The senior management of a licensed or registered person should bear
primary responsibility for ensuring the maintenance of appropriate
N N
standards of conduct and adherence to proper procedures…”
O O
16. As to the imposition of sanctions, the SFC informed the Applicants
P that it proposed the following – P
Q Q
(a) that Cardinalasia, the company, be publicly reprimanded and fined a
sum of HK$1,500,000;
R R
(b) that Edward Lee’s licence to act as a representative in respect of all
S S
regulated activities be suspended for a period of seven months.
T T
U U
- 5 -

A A
17. Both Applicants, through their legal representative, sought - without
B success - to persuade the SFC that its preliminary determinations of culpability were B
without merit. In the result, in decision notices dated 19 July 2021, the Applicants
C C
were informed that the SFC had come to final determinations confirming their
D culpability and that the sanctions earlier proposed were now confirmed. D
E
18. In August 2021, the Applicants filed their Notice of Application for
E
Review with the Tribunal, seeking to set aside the SFC findings of culpability in
F F
respect of both the internal loan arrangements and the cross trades. An amended
G version of that notice was filed with the Tribunal in October 2021. G
H H
19. It was further the case for the Applicants that, should they not be
successful in having the findings of culpability set aside, they sought nevertheless
I I
to challenge the reasonableness of the sanctions imposed upon them.
J J
The regulatory requirement for the investment advisory agreements
K K
20. It is apparent that a material reason why Cardinalasia was chosen as
L L
Investment Adviser to each Fund lay in the fact that it - and Edward Lee – were
M licensed under the Ordinance to carry on Type 4, and especially Type 9, regulated M
activities.
N N
21. At no time between 2014 and 2017 did the companies which
O O
managed each of the Funds hold a Type 9 licence and accordingly it was accepted
P by Edward Lee that in the circumstances it was a regulatory requirement that each P
Fund enter into an investor advisory agreement.
Q Q
22. In order to put matters into context, it should also be said that the
R R
offering memorandum given to potential investors - under the heading of ‘risk
S management’ - spoke of each Fund placing reliance on its management team, this S
team including each Manager and each Investment Adviser:
T T
U U
- 6 -

A A
“Although the Directors have the ultimate authority and responsibility for the
management of the Fund, subject to the parameters determined by the Directors,
B B
the decisions relating to the investment of the Fund’s assets have been delegated
to the Manager which in turn are delegated to the Principal Investment Adviser…”
C C
23. The offering memorandum continued by stressing the fact that the
D D
loss of the Investment Adviser “could materially and negatively impact the value of
the fund.”
E E
F 24. At this early stage of the determination, it is important to point to the F
fact that Edward Lee, the Second Applicant, in the course of his testimony, spoke
G G
of the range of his duties being mapped out not entirely in accordance with the
written terms and conditions of the investment advisory agreements but instead, to
H H
a greater and more decisive extent, being mapped out in accordance with private,
I I
unwritten arrangements reached with Anthony Yeung.
J J
25. It was Edward Lee’s evidence that he considered that the unwritten
K arrangements - seemingly never formalised in any way and never passed on to third K
parties (such as the SFC) - were determinative of the nature and extent of his
L L
obligations. Accordingly, when referred to the citation set out above (in paragraph
22), this being information supplied to potential investors in each Fund, he stated
M M
that this was inaccurate and did not reflect the role of Cardinalasia, the First
N Applicant, in the management of the Funds. However, this inaccuracy in an N
important set of documents was not something which he referred to Anthony Yeung.
O O
26. It was the effect of Edward Lee’s own case therefore that potential
P P
investors reading offering memoranda would have been given incorrect information
Q Q
as to the true role of the Investment Adviser.
R R
The Code of Conduct
S S
27. Of course, as licensed persons under the Ordinance, both Applicants
T were subject to the Code of Conduct, it being a fundamental requirement of the Code T
U U
- 7 -

A A
that licensed persons must deal with each client - each being considered separately
B - in a manner that is fair. In this regard - B
C C
(a) The first general principle of the Code provides that, in conducting
D their licensed business activities, licensed persons (such as the D
Applicants) must act honestly, fairly, in the best interests of their
E E
clients and in accordance with the integrity of the market.
F (b) A complementary principle requires licensed persons to ensure that F
client positions in the market or assets held are adequately
G G
safeguarded.
H (c) The Code further makes reference to conflicts of interest – of direct H
relevance in the present matter - requiring that licensed persons
I I
must seek to avoid such conflicts but, if they cannot be avoided,
they must ensure that their clients are fairly treated.
J J
K The hearing before this Tribunal K
L L
28. Prior to the hearing, the parties agreed in writing, in accordance with
the provisions of the Ordinance, that the Chairman should determine the application
M M
for review alone.
N N
29. The substantive hearing was held over a period of five days in March
O O
2022 with final submissions being made on a day in April 2022. Due to the fact that
P the Chairman was at the time in Australia and was prevented, by reason of prevailing P
Hong Kong Covid 19 regulations, from returning to Hong Kong, the full hearing
Q Q
was conducted by way of video communication: the parties and counsel being in
Hong Kong, the Chairman in Australia.
R R
S S
T T
U U
- 8 -

A A
Underlying legal principles
B B
30. Before looking to the disputed issues before this Tribunal, reference
C C
must be made to certain of the fundamental legal principles that have governed the
context in which the Tribunal has reached its determination of the merits.
D D
E A. This application constitutes a ‘de novo’ hearing E
F F
31. In determining an application for review, it is now well established
that the Tribunal conducts a hearing de novo, undertaking a full merits review. Its
G G
duty, therefore, is not to act as a court of appeal but rather to act as a court of first
H instance. The duty to conduct a hearing de novo extends also to matters of possible H
sanctions.
I I
32. On the basis that this application is conducted as a hearing de novo,
J J
it follows that the burden of proof has at all times remained on the SFC.
K K
B. The standard of proof
L L
33. Section 218 (7) of the Ordinance directs that the standard of proof
M M
required to determine any issue in this application is the standard applicable to civil
N proceedings. Matters must therefore be proved on a balance of probabilities. This N
standard requires that the Tribunal be satisfied that an event has occurred if it
O O
considers that, on the evidence, the occurrence of the event was more likely than
not.
P P
Q C. Expert evidence Q
R R
34. During the course of the hearing, the Tribunal received evidence
which, after consideration, it accepted as being expert evidence. That evidence was
S S
given by Richard Harris. The Tribunal received that evidence because it was likely
T to be outside the knowledge and experience of the Tribunal. That being said, the T
Tribunal recognised that it was entitled to accept or reject all or part of that evidence,
U U
- 9 -

A A
coming to its own conclusion on such matters based on a consideration of all the
B evidence. B
C C
35. As it is, in respect of the expert evidence of Richard Harris, the
Tribunal found it to be balanced, prudent and based on years of experience working
D D
in different financial jurisdictions. As it was put by counsel for the SFC in final
E
submissions: Mr Harris had substantial experience in fund management, stock
E
brokerage and even private banking roles, with exposure to both global and Asian
F F
securities and small-cap stocks in particular. The Tribunal found Mr Harris to be an
G impressive witness, his testimony, as an objective observer, being both balanced and G
considered.
H H
D. Good character
I I
36. During the course of the hearing, it was emphasised that Edward Lee,
J J
the Second Applicant, held academic qualifications of the highest order and, arising
K K
from this, was able to boast an excellent professional record. He was a man therefore
who could be expected to have a sophisticated understanding of how best to protect
L L
the interests of clients in adverse times even if what he did was not entirely orthodox.
M The Tribunal has taken this into account. M
N N
37. It was also stressed that Edward Lee was a man of good character in
that he had not at any earlier time been found guilty of any criminal offences or
O O

The issue of sanctions
L L
M 192. In respect of sanctions, in its notice of final decision, the SFC M
