193. In determining the issue of sanctions, what must be emphasized is
R R
that the culpability of the Applicants has been founded on the fact that, as licensed
S S
persons under the Ordinance, they were bound to a code of conduct, that code
requiring them at all times to act in a manner that sought to protect the legitimate
T T
interests of the investors they represented and, more generally, the integrity of the
U U
- 52 -

A A
market. That gave to the Applicants not only contractual obligations pursuant to the
B investment advisory agreements but independent obligations. The issue of sanctions B
must be viewed in this light.
C C
194. As the Tribunal best understands it, the criticism of the level of the
D D
sanctions imposed was based on the assertion that the role of the Applicants was for
E
all practical purposes severely undermined by the actual authority conferred on them
E
by Anthony Yeung. As it was expressed in final written submissions: “As a result,
F F
the Applicants did not have the power to supersede any decisions already made by
G the Manager and the board of directors”. G
H
195. For the reasons already given in the body of this determination, the
H
Tribunal is satisfied that this approach is misplaced. It was never disputed that
I I
Cardinalasia was at all times acting under delegated authority; as such, Cardinalasia,
J together with Edward Lee, while they had advisory authority to suggest that J
decisions already made should be reconsidered, they had no power to actually set
K K
aside decisions already made by the Manager of each Fund and/or the board of
directors of each Fund. But that is not to denigrate the importance of the authority
L L
vested in Cardinalasia – effectively, therefore, in Edward Lee himself - in terms of
M M
the investment advisory agreements. As the Tribunal has noted earlier, the authority
delegated to Cardinalasia was a mirror of the authority already vested in the
N N
Manager of each fund. It was a proactive authority, one that required the Applicants
O to keep abreast of all investment management matters of any substance and, when O
it was considered prudent to do so, to give advice in respect of such matters (in the
P P
best interests of investors) prior to any final decision being made or, if time did not
Q allow, to give advice in a timely fashion so that any decisions already made, could Q
be reviewed.
R R
196. On the evidence, however, it is apparent that Edward Lee, the
S S
controlling force of Cardinalasia, exercised only the most limited interest in seeking
T to fulfil his obligations, commercial and ethical, as Investment Adviser and, more T
importantly, as a person licensed under the Ordinance. A single example illustrates
U U
- 53 -

A A
the point. When the cross trades were taking place, Edward Lee accepted that the
B Fund Managers who were negotiating those trades harboured considerable B
animosity towards each other. Yet, despite the very broad delegated authority under
C C
which he was supposed to act, Edward Lee said that he did nothing: the corporate
D parties appeared to know what they wanted and he was satisfied to leave it at that. D
It would appear that any potential danger of the existing animosity warping the
E E
fairness of proceedings was not his concern.

197. Of equal significance, and concerning the two crises which were the
G subject of this application for review, namely, the internal loan arrangements and G
the cross trades, there is no independent evidence by way of paper advice or
H H
electronic messaging that the Applicants took any action of substance (or indeed of
any kind) to discharge their delegated but nevertheless highly important obligations.
I I
Arising out of these two crises, especially as there was, in respect of the first matter,
J
a real danger apparently of one or more of the Funds going into liquidation, if the
J
applicants had been discharging their delegated duties, one would have expected a
K K
relatively large volume of evidence recording advice given. But there was none.
L L
198. It could not have been a case of the Applicants not having the
M M
experience or the wit to understand the true nature of the authority delegated to them
in the various investment advisory agreements and how, as licensed persons under
N N
the Ordinance, they should set about their obligations.
O O
199. Edward Lee, it appears, had an enviable education in matters of
P P
finance and equally enviable experience. As such, surely, he would have ensured
that he understood the true nature and extent of the advisory agreements and his
Q Q
obligations under them and would, at all times, have been aware of the broader
R
responsibilities placed on his shoulders as a licensed person. Regrettably, however,
R
if he did understand the true nature and extent of his obligations, there is little or no
S S
evidence that he bothered to act in accordance with that understanding.
T T
U U
- 54 -

A A
200. The clear importance of an investment adviser in protecting the
B interests of investors lies in the simple, single fact that the person so appointed acts B
in an independent way, even if acting under delegated powers, employing his or her
C C
knowledge and understanding of financial matters in order to ensure the best
D interests of investors even if, in giving advice, that advice is contrary to the intended D
wishes, or decisions actually made, of those higher in the delegated chain of
E E
management. In the judgement of the Tribunal, Edward Lee, appears not to have
been too concerned to ensure that he discharged his fiduciary obligations in a timely
F F
and independent manner.
G G
201. As the Tribunal has noted in earlier decisions, sanctions that are
H H
imposed are not designed to punish, in the sense, that is, of punishment under
criminal law. That they may cause hurt to individuals is not the point. The point is
I I
that the sanctions are designed to be defensive, that is, they are designed to protect
J J
the essential integrity of the market and the investors in that market. As such, there
may be occasions - and the Tribunal considers this to be one of them - when the
K K
maintenance of public confidence can only be attained by removing a party from
L the market for a period of time of some length. L
M M
202. In the present case, on a consideration of all relevant evidence, the
Tribunal is of the view that the period of suspension imposed by the SFC was
N N
insufficient in order to protect the market.
O O
203. In the view of the Tribunal, it is important that others acting in the
P P
same capacity should appreciate that the role of an investment adviser is a role of
real substance.
Q Q
R 204. In coming to its determination on an entirely de novo basis, without R
any consideration for the fact that the SFC had imposed a period of suspension of
S S
seven months only, the Tribunal would have considered a period of suspension of
12 months to have been appropriate. But, looking to the fact that a period of
T T
suspension 12 months would be very significantly greater, and being satisfied that
U U
- 55 -

A A
the protection of the market will not be undermined, the Tribunal has determined
B that a period of nine months suspension is appropriate. B
C C
205. The Tribunal, therefore, orders that Edward Lee be suspended for a
period of nine months.
D D

Tribunal is satisfied, again viewing matters de novo, that they are fair and reasonable

and there will be no change in respect of those orders.

Costs
H H
207. In light of the fact that this application for review by both Applicants
I I
has been dismissed, and there being no persuasive reasons to come to any other
determination, the Tribunal is satisfied that costs must follow the event. Accordingly,
J J
costs are to be paid by the Applicants, the obligation being joint and several.
K K
208. Having regard to the detailed nature of much of the evidence, the
L L
Tribunal is further satisfied that the SFC should be entitled to a certificate for two
counsel.
M M
N N
O O
P P
(Mr Michael Hartmann)
Q (Chairman) Q
R R
Mr Edwin Choy, SC leading Mr Philip C.L. Wong, instructed by Messrs Michael
S Li & Co S
for the Applicants
T T
Ms Sara Tong, Counsel and Ms Esther Mak, Counsel instructed by the SFC
for the Respondent
U U
- 56 -

title: SFAT Application No. 3/2021 (Ruling)
text:
Application No. 3 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 208 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
PAN TIANYU Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Date of Ruling: 23 August 2022
____________________
RULING
____________________

A A
1. In December 2020, the Securities and Futures Commission (“the SFC”),
B the Respondent, issued a notice pursuant to sections 204 and 205 of the Securities B
and Futures Ordinance, Cap 571 (“the Ordinance”) prohibiting CNI Securities
C C
Group Limited, a company licensed under the Ordinance, from in any way dealing
in the assets of the Applicant up to a value of some HK$50.964 million.
D D
E E
2. At the time when the notice was issued, the SFC had reason to believe
that a group of traders, including the Applicant, had acted in concert in a pre-
F F
arranged scheme to inflate the share price of a company called Mansion
G International Holdings Ltd to an artificially high level, following that with an abrupt G
disposal of the shares, the great majority being sold in the span of just one day. The
H H
SFC had reason to suspect that the group of traders may have committed offences
under the Ordinance by way of false trading, price rigging and/or market
I I
manipulation.
J J
3. Subsequent to the imposition of the notice, the Applicant requested
K K
the SFC to withdraw or vary the notice so that he could receive part or all of his
L ‘frozen’ capital. The application was refused. L
M M
4. In the result, on 19 August 2021 the Applicant filed his notice of
N application for review of that refusal, seeking a review of the SFC decision and a N
declaration to the effect that the SFC had insufficient evidence to show that the
O O
Applicant’s share dealings had been contrary to any provisions of the Ordinance.
P P
5. The notice of application for review was filed on behalf of the
Q
Applicant by his then solicitors, Angela Ho & Associates, the application being set
Q
down for hearing on 6 June 2022.
R R
S 6. Prior to that date, however, by letter dated 26 May 2022, the S
Applicant’s solicitors informed the Tribunal that it was no longer able to represent
T T
the Applicant as it had been unable to contact the Applicant in order to obtain
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
instructions from him. In the result, the Applicant’s solicitors informed the Tribunal
B that it would not appear in any representative capacity at the hearing. B
C C
7. Upon receipt of this information, the Tribunal issued an order
D addressed directly to the Applicant requiring him within 14 days to confirm in D
writing whether or not he intended to proceed with his application for review and,
E E
if so, to provide contact information and to lodge with the Tribunal, and serve also
on the SFC, his written submissions. The order provided that, if there was no
F F
response from the Applicant within 14 days of the order being served upon him, his
G application would be treated as withdrawn. G
H H
8. In due course, the SFC supplied proof that the order (together with its
I Chinese translation) had been served at the Applicant’s address in the Mainland on I
2 July 2022. There was no response received from him within the required period
J J
of 14 days; indeed, it does not appear that the Applicant has at any time up to the
date of this ruling sought to further his case before the Tribunal.
K K
L
9. The SFC has now made an application for an order that the
L
Applicant’s application for review be treated as withdrawn. The Tribunal is satisfied
M M
that the Applicant has taken no steps to pursue his application. In the judgment of
N the Tribunal, the application for review is accordingly to be treated as withdrawn. N
O O
10. In light of that order, the SFC has sought an order for costs, such costs
to be assessed on an indemnity basis.
P P
Q 11. Section 223 (1)(b) of the Ordinance provides that the Tribunal may, Q
in relation to an application for review, award costs to any party to the review.
R R
Section 223(3) provides that the award of costs by the Tribunal is to be governed by
order 62 of the rules of the High Court. Order 62 itself1 provides that, in determining
S S
costs, a court may, in any case in which it thinks it fit to do so, direct that costs shall
T T
1 Specifically, Order 62, Rule 28(3).
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
be taxed on an indemnity basis. That this Tribunal has the power to award costs on
B an indemnity basis is clear and is now well established in its practice. B
C C
12. As to the exercise of this Tribunal’s discretion, definitive guidance is
D to be obtained from Choy Yee Chun (The Representative of the estate of Chan Pui D
Yiu) v Bond Star Development Ltd [1997] HKLRD 1327. On the basis of that
E E
authority, it is settled that indemnity costs can properly be ordered when
proceedings have been initiated maliciously, in an oppressive manner or for an
F F
ulterior motive.
G G
13. The discretion vested in this Tribunal is therefore a broad one, the key
H H
question being: is such an order in the circumstances an appropriate order? Or, put
I another way, having regard to all relevant circumstances, is such an order justified? I
J J
14. The SFC has founded its application for enhanced costs on a number
of grounds which may be summarised as follows: first, that the application itself
K K
was not made in good faith, that it was vexatious or made with an ulterior motive in
L
mind; second, that the Applicant had made an abrupt decision to abandon his own
L
application, removing himself from any contact with his own legal representatives,
M M
with the SFC and with this Tribunal, third, that his actions generally had constituted
N an affront to the integrity of the Tribunal. N
O O
15. As to the first ground, namely, that the application had patently not
been made in good faith, the Tribunal has difficulty accepting that this has been
P P
demonstrated. The application, on the evidence available, was not, for example, a
Q Q
delaying exercise. In this regard, it is to be remembered that the Applicant was
seeking the release of his own funds. He had attempted by way of negotiation to
R R
obtain that release or to have the imposition modified but had been unsuccessful.
S The only avenue open to him to seek full, or partial, release of a very substantial S
sum of money was therefore by way of his application. Whether the Applicant’s
T T
case in law was at all times demonstrably weak, so much so that it must have been
made in bad faith is not a matter that this Tribunal can now determine. In such
U U
- 4 -
V V

A A
circumstances - absent other compelling evidence - it is difficult to find that the
B Applicant, when he made his application, did so in bad faith. B
C C
16. As to the second ground, namely that the Applicant had made an
D abrupt decision to abandon his own application, it is to be remembered that, in doing D
so, the applicant was also abandoning his claim for the return to him of funds to
E E
which he believed himself to be entitled. In the view of the Tribunal, abandonment
by a party, either in part or in whole, of a claim cannot, of itself, constitute conduct
F F
which smacks of bad faith. It is often a realisation simply that, on balance, the
G likelihood of success is limited. G
H H
17. As to the third ground, there is no substantive evidence before the
I Tribunal that the Applicant has intentionally acted so as to insult the Tribunal and/or I
to undermine its process. He may have ‘washed his hands’ of the whole process,
J J
refusing to cooperate with the SFC investigators. But in the present circumstances,
the Tribunal does not see that his abandonment of his own claim, even when read
K K
with the other matters outlined by the SFC, is sufficient to warrant an enhanced
L
order.
L
M M
18. In the circumstances of this particular case, the Tribunal is not
N persuaded that persuasive grounds exist for awarding costs on an indemnity basis. N
Costs will therefore be awarded on a party and party basis.
O O
19. That said, the Tribunal accepts that, having regard to the complexity
P P
of the matters that were at issue, a certificate for two counsel is deserved and will
Q Q
be granted.
R R
20. The Tribunal therefore makes the following orders –
S S
(1) That the application for review is treated as withdrawn.
T T
U U
- 5 -
V V

title: SFAT Application No. 4/2021 (Ruling)

IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL

Application No. 4 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL

title: SFAT Application No. 4/2020
text:
Application No. 4 of 2020
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
YI SHUN DA CAPITAL LIMITED Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Date of Hearing: 26 April 2021
Date of Determination: 19 October 2021
____________________
DETERMINATION
________________________

A A
Introduction
B B
1. At all times relevant to this determination, Imperial Sierra Group Holdings
C C
Limited (“Imperial Sierra” or “the Group”) carried on business as commercial property
consultants, its operations being centred in the Pearl River Delta area of the Mainland.
D D
2. The principal revenue of the Company was obtained from advisory services
E E
provided on a project-to-project basis to a limited number of customers, its top five
F customers in 2016 accounting for some 76% of revenue. F
G G
3. By contrast, property management services, which provided a regular and
recurring income stream, accounted for only 1.1% of its 2016 revenue1.
H H
I 4. The founder of Imperial Sierra was Yip Wik, Aric (“Aric Yip”) who was I
Chairman of the Board, an executive director and the controlling shareholder.
J J
5. In December 2016, Imperial Sierra, having resolved to seek a listing on the
K K
Main Board of the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong (“the Stock Exchange”), appointed the
Applicant in this matter, Yi Shun Da Capital Limited, then known as Zhaobangji
L L
International Capital Limited2, as its ‘sole sponsor, global coordinator, bookrunner and lead
M manager’3 in order to guide it through the necessary listing procedures. M
N N
6. The Applicant was at all relevant times licensed pursuant to the provisions
of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Chapter 571 (“the Ordinance”), to engage in Type
O O
1 and Type 6 regulated activities; Type 1 being dealing in securities, Type 6 being advising
P on matters of corporate finance. P
Q Q
R
1 This data is taken from the Company’s draft prospectus submitted to the Listing Division of the Stock
R
Exchange of Hong Kong in March 2017.
S 2 The Applicant was known as Zhaobangji International Capital Limited from November 2015 until S
December 2017 and as Well Link International Capital Limited from December 2017 to August 2018 when
it adopted the present name of Yi Shun Da Capital Limited.
T T
3 This is taken from the English translation of the contract of appointment between Imperial Sierra and the
Applicant.
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
7. On 30 March 2017, the Applicant, in its role as the sole sponsor, submitted
B an application (including a draft prospectus and draft accountant’s report) for the listing of B
Imperial Sierra to the Listing Division of the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong.
C C
8. In vetting the listing application, a number of fundamental concerns were
D D
raised in respect of ‘financial issues’, the essence of those concerns being that, in order to
show a stronger financial position, there may have been a circular flow of funds.
E E
F 9. Exchanges then took place in terms of which the Applicant sought – F
unsuccessfully – to demonstrate that it had had exercised due diligence in respect of those
G G
concerns.
H H
10. In January 2020 – by which time Imperial Sierra’s application for listing
I had lapsed – the Respondent in this matter, the Securities and Futures Commission (“the I
SFC”), informed the Applicant that it proposed to bring disciplinary action against the
J
Applicant for a failure on its part – at the time of submitting the application for listing – to
J
exercise reasonable due diligence in its role as the sole sponsor. The proposed action was
K K
based on three areas of contention, each of them to be considered together and each being
related to a possible circular flow of funds or similar scheme. The three issues may be
L L
broadly summarized as follows –
M M
(a) That over the three-year period before the listing application, a very high
N N
percentage of payments to Imperial Sierra had not been made by the debtors
themselves but by third parties on their behalf. To give an indication of the
O O
extent of this practice, in the first two years of the three-year period, third-
P party payments in each year exceeded 50% of the Group’s total revenue P
while, in the third year, they came close to 40% of revenue. In this regard,
Q
and taking into account the very high value of the third-party payments, it
Q
would be fair to say that, in the three-year lead-up period, third-party
R R
payments came close to being the dominant payment method. In the view
of the SFC, this was, in any set of circumstances, highly unusual and
S S
demanding of explanation. As it was put by the SFC, the concern arose out
T of the fact that substantial third-party payments may have been used “to T
disguise the original source of funds and facilitate a deceptive or fraudulent
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
scheme”. Despite this, however, it appeared on the evidence that only
B minimal enquiries had been made by the Applicant’s transaction team. For B
example, none of the major customers of Imperial Sierra who had arranged
C C
for third-party payments, or agreed to them, were asked to explain why they
had cooperated with the arrangements.
D D
(b) That there had in addition been two ‘suspicious sets of transactions’ pointing
E E
to the possibility of a circular flow of funds. By way of illustration, on
F 24 June 2015 a company called Guangdong Qitian, acting as a third party, F
had made a payment of RMB2.3 million to Imperial Sierra on behalf of a
G G
major customer, the Wise Group. Two days later, Imperial Sierra remitted
RMB2 million back to Guangdong Qitian by way of a personal loan
H H
advanced by Aric Yip, Imperial Sierra’s Chairman of the Board and
I controlling shareholder. I
J J
(c) That over the same period of time, Aric Yip had entered into concerning
financial arrangements with various individuals – termed ‘acquaintances’ –
K K
a number of whom may have had a connection with the third-party payers.
L In this regard, evidence showed that in the financial years ending 2014, 2015 L
and 2016, Aric Yip had withdrawn amounts of HK$6.3 million,
M M
HK$16.5 million and HK$18.8 million. In addition, as at 31 January 2017,
there was evidence of further withdrawals of some HK$35 million. These
N N
funds had been used to facilitate a number of financial arrangements
O (apparently for loan/investment purposes) between Aric Yip and a total of O
11 acquaintances. By way of illustrating the potential significance of these
P
financial arrangements –
P
Q Q
(i) One of the acquaintances which had been in receipt of funds from
R Aric Yip, Guangzhou Meijin, had made third-party payments to R
Imperial Sierra, doing so on behalf of a company called Guanghzou
S S
Mingdu.
T T
U U
- 4 -
V V

A A
(ii) Another acquaintance which had been in receipt of funds from Aric
B Yip. Gaungzhou Chengzhi, had been beneficially owned by a man B
named Wang Jing who was also the beneficial owner of a company
C C
called Foshan Nanfang, that company being a customer of Imperial
Sierra.
D D
E 11. According to the SFC, these matters (and others) had not been disclosed in E
the draft prospectus and therefore, if the prospectus had been approved, would not have
F F
been known to potential investors. In the result, so it was asserted by the SFC, it appeared
that the Applicant had failed to comply with all necessary regulatory requirements
G G
applicable to the conduct of a sponsor, particularly those contained in the Code of Conduct4
H and Practice Note 21 of the Listing Rules (Due Diligence by Sponsors in respect of Initial H
Listing Applications). The SFC was further of the view that it appeared that the Applicant
I
had breached a number of provisions of the Code of Conduct for Persons Licensed by or
I
Registered with the SFC5 as well as the Code of Conduct applicable to corporate finance
J J
advisers.
K K
12. The Applicant’s written submissions6 made in response were not accepted
L L
and, by a notice dated 9 June 2020, the SFC gave its final decision which was to the
following effect, namely, that the Applicant was found guilty of misconduct and ruled not
M M
to be a fit and proper person to remain licensed7. That, in addition the Applicant was
N publicly reprimanded and fined HK$4.5 million pursuant to the provisions of section 194 N
of the Ordinance.
O O
13. In July 2020, the Applicant sought a review of the SFC decision. It was
P P
agreed that the matter would be heard by the Chairman sitting alone.
Q Q
4 Paragraph 17 of the Code sets out the duties of sponsors; paragraphs 17.2 – 17.7 setting out a sponsor’s
obligations in respect of the exercise of due diligence.
R R
5 The SFC cited possible breaches of General Principles 2 and 7 and paragraphs 12.1, 17.2(b) ,17.4(a), 17.4(b),
17.6(a), 17.6(b), 17.6(c), 17.6(e) and 17.6(f) of the Code of Conduct.
S S
6 The written submissions in response were dated 26 March 2020.
7 The Applicant had, in fact, ceased to accept new sponsor work and therefore accepted a condition on its
T T
licence to the effect that “it shall not act as a sponsor in respect of an application for the listing on a
recognized stock market of any securities”.
U U
- 5 -
V V

A A
The issues defining the SFC case
B B
14. As indicated earlier, the disciplinary sanctions imposed by the SFC have at
C C
all times been limited to an alleged failure by the Applicant, as sole sponsor, to perform all
reasonable due diligence in respect of its original listing application. This failure of due
D D
diligence related to the three areas of concern outlined above which may have indicated a
managed circular flow of funds.
E E
F 15. Imperial Sierra was never successfully listed. The shareholding public F
suffered no loss. Accordingly, the primary concern of the SFC was at all times limited to
G G
the single issue of whether the Applicant, in discharging its duties as sole sponsor and
presenting the draft prospectus, had performed all reasonable due diligence in respect of
H H
the financial issues that were, on their face, troubling and which, in the exercise of proper
I due diligence, demanded a more thorough investigation than the one undertaken by the I
Applicant. The SFC did not suggest that payments had been fictitious. The concern was
J
whether – with all three areas of concern being taken into account – there had been a
J
legitimate risk of some form of circular flow of funds which required a more incisive
K K
investigation than the one conducted by the Applicant.
L L
The underlying basis of the application for review
M M
16. It was the Applicant’s case on review that it had not failed to conduct due
N N
diligence as alleged. While there may have been a significant number of third-party
payments made to Imperial Sierra in the three years before its intended listing, payments
O O
of that nature did exist in the PRC. A different commercial landscape existed there as
P evidenced by the fact that a number of earlier listings on the Hong Kong Stock Market had P
revealed the receipt of third-party payments (although seemingly nothing as extensive as
Q
the third-party payments made to Imperial Sierra).
Q
R R
17. Concerning the fact that in this present matter, third-party payments were
not an occasional happening but amounted to a dominant practice, counsel for the
S S
Applicant emphasised that no allegations had been made by the SFC, nor had any evidence
T been led to suggest that the third-party payments had been fictitious. There had been no T
allegation made by the SFC of fraud. Counsel for the Applicant submitted that the SFC
U U
- 6 -
V V

A A
complaint appeared to be based on the contention that the Applicant’s transaction team had
B not investigated why the third-party payments had been made. This question, however, was B
simply not relevant to the exercise of due diligence. The SFC had not suggested, nor was
C C
there any logical reason to suggest, that third-party payments to Imperial Sierra were in
any way inherently suspicious and ought to have caused concern to the Applicant. What
D D
mattered, it was argued, was the fact of receipt of payments and there was no argument as
to this defining issue. Any suggestion, therefore, that these third-party payments had been
E E
engineered in order to somehow demonstrate that Imperial Sierra’s revenues were greater
F then in fact they were had to be purely speculative and not a matter, therefore, deserving F
of the sort of exhaustive investigation advanced by the SFC.
G G
18. Similarly, in respect of the two ‘suspicious transactions’, it was submitted
H H
on behalf of the Applicant, that there was nothing inherently suspicious in either of them.
I If there was a suggestion that in some manner they were part of an engineered flow of funds I
designed to give the appearance that Imperial Sierra’s revenues were greater that in fact
J
they were, any such suggestion was again purely speculative.
J
K K
19. As to Aric Yip’s various finance arrangements with 11 acquaintances, their
details were revealed and in addition the Applicant’s transaction team had set out details
L L
of its due diligence work to the SFC in a PowerPoint presentation. These financial
M arrangements funded by Aric Yip were not capable of giving rise to any concern so long M
as the withdrawals had been properly booked in the company accounts: which they were.
N N
On the basis that the purpose of due diligence was to ascertain the genuineness, and the
existence, of the finance arrangements – and this having been done – the circumstances did
O O
not require further investigation by the Applicant’s transaction team.
P P
20. The SFC, of course, had not at any time alleged fraud (which would
Q
encompass an engineered flow of transactions in order to give a false picture to potential
Q
share purchasers) for the simple reason that it had no grounds for doing so. Similarly, the
R R
SFC had not alleged that any of the transactions were necessarily fictitious on the basis
again that it had no grounds for doing so. The SFC was not the principal investigator. The
S S
job of verification lay with the Applicant. As the Tribunal has understood it, it has instead
T at all times been its position that, in the particular circumstances of this case, with third- T
party payments being a dominant practice, sufficient areas of concern should have
U U
- 7 -
V V

A A
presented themselves to the Applicant’s transaction team during the course of
B investigations to require it, in the discharge of its due diligence responsibilities, to look B
deeper into those matters. As it was put by counsel for the SFC, it then became imperative
C C
for the Applicant to understand and then critically assess the reasons for the payments. This
would have involved an understanding of the relationships between the third-party payers
D D
and the Imperial Sierra customers. It was accepted by the SFC that, if it had done so, it may
have come to the conclusion, one based on a detailed and rational assessment, that there
E E
had been no circular flow of funds or anything of a similar nature to cause concern. Equally,
F it may have come to the conclusion that the concerns had been justified. The fact is, F
however, that it had failed to conduct reasonable due diligence in respect of these matters
G G
- and in that lay its culpability.
H H
21. It is of course fundamental that, when new shares are listed on the Stock
I Exchange and presented to the market, material issues that may give rise to concern on the I
part of potential purchasers should be dealt with in a clear and incisive manner so that
J
potential purchasers know what risks, if any, they face and, if so, the nature and extent of
J
those risks. For a prospectus to do otherwise endangers the integrity of the market.
K K
22. In the present case, as the Tribunal has understood it, it has been the SFC’s
L L
position that the Applicant must have known that the issue of third-party payments and
M other associated transactions would cause concern. Indeed, in its draft prospectus, it set M
aside several passages in order to deal with the matter. In doing so, however, it failed in an
N N
incisive manner to investigate the issues that raised concern, permitting itself too often to
rely on generalised statements by representatives of Imperial Sierra. It is on this basis that
O O
the SFC came to a finding that the Applicant was culpable, basing this finding in large
P measure on the due diligence provisions in the Code of Conduct, paragraph 17.6(c) P
requiring that –
Q Q
“A sponsor should not merely accept statements and representations made
R
and documents produced by a listing applicant or its directors at face value.
R
Depending on the nature and source of the information and the context in
S which the information is given, the sponsor should perform verification S
procedures that are appropriate in the circumstances, such as reviewing
source documents, enquiring of knowledgeable persons or obtaining
T T
independently sourced information. Where the sponsor becomes aware of
circumstances that may cast doubt on information provided to it or
U U
- 8 -
V V

A A
otherwise indicate a potential problem or risk, the sponsor should undertake
additional due diligence to ascertain the truth and completeness of the
B B
matter and information concerned.”
C C
The manner in which this Tribunal must determine this application
D D
23. It is now well settled that the Tribunal must determine the application de
E novo, that is, as a full merits review, acting as if it was the original decision-maker. That E
being the case, the burden of proof remains on the SFC. That burden is the burden applied
F F
in civil proceedings; namely, proof on a balance of probabilities.
G G
Principles guiding the duties of sponsors
H H
24. The role of a sponsor has been considered on a number of occasions by this
I
Tribunal. In Sun Hung Kai International Limited v SFC8, the Tribunal noted that, when a
I
corporate financial adviser takes on the role of sponsor –
J J
“… it accepts a dual obligation, that is, to the client of course but equally to
K K
the Stock Exchange in respect of the application for listing and to investors
generally in respect of the prospectus. What must be underscored is that the
L L
sponsor is more than a mere servant of an applicant and must act with
independent professionalism in ensuring that all information placed before
M
the Stock Exchange and investors generally is fully, fairly and accurately
M
presented.” [emphasis added]
N N
25. That is why, in undertaking its role, a sponsor must examine the accuracy
O O
and completeness of representations made to it by the representatives of the company
seeking listing and do so with the necessary degree of professional scepticism. The
P P
requirement to adopt an attitude of professional scepticism places on the shoulders of a
sponsor the requirement to make a critical assessment of relevant information, doing so
Q Q
with a questioning mind and – of central importance in the present case – being alert to
R information that brings into question the reliability of relevant statements and R
representations.
S S
T T
8 Application No. 3 of 2013.
U U
- 9 -
V V

A A
26. Where a sponsor becomes aware of circumstances that may cast doubt on
B information provided to it or otherwise indicate a potential problem or risk, the sponsor is B
then obliged to undertake additional due diligence in order, hopefully, to arrive at an
C C
accurate understanding.

be problematic, undue reliance on management representations, especially those that are
E E
bland and without detail, cannot be regarded as a proper discharge of reasonable due
F diligence. F

28. This is not to suggest that a sponsor is required to provide counsel of
perfection. The method by which a sponsor discharges its dual obligation is by employing
H H
due skill and care, tempered always with a healthy but rational degree of scepticism, doing
I so in the best interests of its client and – importantly – the integrity of the market. I

Were the SFC concerns unfounded in that they were ‘speculative’?

of a rational degree of scepticism does not mean that every ‘speculated’ risk, no matter how
N immaterial, must be chased down every hole. Accordingly, the Applicant was not culpable N
of any failure of due diligence.

fact that this indirect form of payment, rather than being an occasional form of payment

“In an orderly and transparent market investors must be able to place trust,

first, in the fact that the listing of a company has been founded by the Stock

that the information contained in the prospectus is also full and accurate.
The route to ensuring such trust must rest principally on the sponsor, the
G

on the conduct of an objective, professional and scrupulous investigation of
H all material relevant to the listing and the initial public offering; in short, on H

B considering whether the SFC has demonstrated a lack of reasonable due diligence on the B
part of the Applicant, the Tribunal has been drawn to the following findings –
C C
(a) In light of the fact that third-party payments made through a variety of third
D D
parties (including by the wife of a senior manager of Imperial Sierra)

magnitude alone, appeared to go far beyond mere happenstance, the
G G
Applicant’s transaction team was left with no option other than to conduct
a suitable investigation into the matter, doing so in order, if possible, to
H H
explain how the practice had arisen, why it had prevailed over the three years
I and why it was a legitimate practice, not one designed perhaps to give a false I
impression to the market.
J J
(b) A suitable investigation into the matter, however, if it was to seek an answer
K K
as to the legitimacy of the practice, demanded more than answers which,
while superficially they appeared to give an answer, in reality gave no
L L

M Applicant’s transaction team was clearly aware of the fact that the issue of M
third-party payments would be of concern to the market: hence the passages
N N
in the draft prospectus to which reference has been made earlier. The
members of the transaction team must therefore have understood that the

further explanation, they could equally be very inconvenient or answers to

the effect that such payments were made by way of private arrangements
Q

demanded more questions. In addition, the fact that a number of third parties
were simply unwilling to explain why they had made third-party payments

T Tribunal, as set out above, due diligence demanded further and more T

the funds was revealed.
I I
74. As it was put by counsel for the SFC, given that some of the acquaintances
J
were third-party payers or entities with connections to Imperial Sierra’s customers – which
J
inflated the risk of a circular flow of funds between Imperial Sierra and its customers – it
K K
was especially important that the true nature and purpose of the transactions be verified.
L L
75. In respect of its due diligence work, it was the Applicant’s case that it had
M interviewed six of the 11 acquaintances, obtaining confirmation of the reasons for each M
transaction, the amounts involved and the fact of their independence from Imperial Sierra
N N
and its customers.
O O
76. In an interview with the representative of one of the companies that received
P funding, Guangdong Junfeng, it was said that the money had been obtained for ‘business P
needs’. The same reason was given by the representative of Guangzhou Meijin: the funds
Q
had been obtained for ‘business needs’. Again, answers were given in the very broadest of
Q
terms: effectively saying nothing.
R R
77. In addition, as part of the due diligence process, the Applicant had arranged
S S
for background searches to be conducted related to ten of the acquaintances. In respect of
T these searches, it accepted that there had been discrepancies in two of the interviews but T
these were considered to be trivial. Counsel for the SFC disagreed, saying that the
U U
- 23 -
V V

A A
discrepancies had related to the ‘primary nature of the finance arrangements’. As such, they
B demanded a follow-up. B
C C
78. One of the difficulties facing the Applicant was that, in the course of an
interview, Michael Wong, one of the Applicant’s senior members at the time, accepted that
D D
the transaction team should have had a clearer picture of the nature and extent of the loans
being advanced to acquaintances. He further accepted that, in order to obtain that clearer
E E
picture, there should have been steps taken to look at source material; for example, to
F confirm whether loans have been repaid or not. Mr Wong accepted that the transaction F
team had not obtained even basic underlying documentation such as bank-in the slips. In
G G
the result, he admitted, matters had been left more opaque than they should have been.
H H
79. On behalf of the Applicant, support was also sought from the fact that the
I draft accountant’s report confirmed that no impropriety or irregularity had been detected I
and that, in its view, the financial statements provided a true and fair picture of Imperial
J
Sierra’s financial affairs. That was further evidence, it was submitted, to demonstrate that
J
the sort of in-depth investigation required by the SFC had never been necessary.
K K
80. As to the importance of the accountant’s report in respect of the Applicant’s
L L
obligation to conduct due diligence, the Tribunal has made reference to this earlier in this
M report: see paragraph 50. M
N N
81. It was submitted on behalf of the Applicant that the fact that finance
arrangements had been funded by Aric Yip did not, and should not, have given rise to any
O O
concern so long as the withdrawals were properly booked in the necessary accounts and
P there was no concern, therefore, as to their genuineness. That being the case, whether the P
acquaintances were all able to explain fully the reasons or purposes of the relevant
Q
arrangements should not have given rise to any concern or require further investigation.
Q
As it was said by counsel for the Applicant: “it was unnecessary for the Applicant to
R R
conduct a microscopic examination of all relevant documents and to conduct enquiries just
because certain names were the same and/or similar. Such burden was unduly onerous.
S S
Such unsubstantiated speculation did not need to be [set out] in the draft prospectus.”
T T
U U
- 24 -
V V

A A
82. The Tribunal does not agree. If, looking at the overall picture, there was
B legitimate reason for concern that there may have been a circular flow of funds – as there B
was - then all relevant matters had to be considered and that included the substantial
C C
loan/investment transactions between Aric Yip, the controlling shareholder of Imperial
Sierra, and various acquaintances who themselves had, or may have had, some connection
D D
to the parties involved in the possible circular flow of funds. In such circumstances, the
true issue to be determined was not whether payments out and payments received were
E E
duly recorded but what was effect of this flow of finance.
F F
83. It was the SFC finding, that there had been a failure to obtain and review
G G
the agreements themselves and relevant bank records; there had been a failure to follow up
on unsatisfactory or incorrect responses provided by acquaintances and, of importance,
H H
there had been a failure to state in the draft prospectus that four of the acquaintances were
I either third-party payers or were connected with the top five customers of Imperial Sierra. I
By way of illustration –
J J
(a) One of the acquaintances, Guangzhou Meijin, which had been in receipt of
K K
funds from Aric Yip, had made third-party payments to Imperial Sierra on
behalf of one of Imperial Sierra’s customers, Guangzhou Mingdu.
L L
M (b) Another acquaintance which had been in receipt of funds from Aric Yip, M
Guangzhou Chengzhi, was beneficially owned by a man named Wang Jing
N N
who was also the beneficial owner of Foshan Nanfang, a customer of
Imperial Sierra.
O O
P 84. Again, the Tribunal is satisfied that there was a failure to exercise due P
diligence on the part of the Applicant.
Q Q
The findings of the Tribunal
R R
85. By way of a general determination, therefore, the Tribunal is satisfied to the
S S
T T
U U
- 25 -
V V

A A
required standard that in respect of the three areas of concern outlined by the SFC, there
B was a failure to exercise reasonable due diligence on the part of the Applicant12. B
C C
The issue of sanctions
D D
(A) The objection to identifying the Applicant by the name ‘Well Link’
E E
86. Unusually, in respect of sanctions, the first matter raised by the Applicant
F was concerned with the wording of the SFC draft press release, more particularly, with the F
fact that the press release cited the three names by which the Applicant has been known
G G
since late 2015 through until the present time, one of those names being ‘Well Link
International Capital’.
H H
I 87. As indicated in the second footnote to this determination, the Applicant was I
known as ‘Zhaobangji International Capital’ until mid-December 2017. It then changed its
J
name to ‘Well Link International Capital’, the name being changed to ‘Yi Shun Da Capital’
J
– its present name – in August 2018.
K K
88. On behalf of the Applicant, counsel said that no objection was taken to the
L L
inclusion in the press release of the name ‘Zhaobangji International Capital’ as that was the
M name by which the Applicant was known when the sponsorship work was undertaken. M
Similarly, because that was the Applicant’s current name, no objection was taken to the
N N
inclusion in the press release of the name, ‘Yi Shun Da Capital’.
O O
89. However, objection was taken to the citation of the name ‘Well Link
P International Capital’ on the basis that this was the name by which the Applicant was P
known after the impugned sponsorship had been completed and was, in addition, a name
Q
abandoned before the present SFC proceedings.
Q
R R
90. In the opinion of the Tribunal, the Applicant’s contention is misconceived.
S S
T T
12 Although the Tribunal has not set out in detail its analysis of the various schedules/tables presented to it
during the hearing, that analysis has backed its findings as to culpability.
U U
- 26 -
V V

A A
It is to be remembered that sanctions permitted under the Ordinance are defensive in nature
B and not penal. Their purpose is to defend the integrity of the market and, by use of B
appropriate sanctions, to ensure that the particular harm is not repeated either by the
C C
offender in question or by any other operators in the market. A threat to the market can
only effectively be countered if the source of that threat is clearly identified. Fundamental
D D
to this purpose is the requirement that in each case the offender in question is fully and
accurately identified. It must follow that, for that to be done, the name – or names – by
E E
which the offender is, or has been, known must be made known to the market. If an offender
F has changed the name by which it is known then, for the offender itself to be identified, F
that change of name must also be made known. It is to be emphasised that those persons
G G
who did business with the Applicant when it was known as Well Link were dealing with
the same corporation now known as Yi Shun Da.
H H
I (B) The sufficiency of a public reprimand I
J
91. It was Applicant’s contention that a public reprimand constituted a
J
sufficient sanction in the circumstances of this case and that a fine was unwarranted. In this
K K
regard, it was submitted that, as a matter of principle, a public reprimand was appropriate
when, as in the present case, the misconduct had not affected the market, indeed would not
L L
have been generally even known to the market, and when adequate measures had been
M taken to address the identified failings. M
N N
92. It was further urged on the Tribunal that the Applicant had relied heavily
upon the expertise and experience of its transaction team, that team being led by a person
O O
with more than 25 years’ relevant experience.
P P
93. That said, as the Tribunal sees it, it is inevitable that sponsors will set up an
Q
operational team in order to bring a listing application to fruition. It does not mean that the
Q
sponsor can wash its hands of responsibility. No matter how experienced the operational
R R
team may be, it remains the responsibility of the sponsor throughout to ensure that the team
fully undertakes its duties and complies with its responsibilities. It is also to be emphasised
S S
that, on the face of it, the failings were broader ‘team’ failings. This is not a case in which
T one ‘rogue’ member failed to live up to his or her obligations. T
U U
- 27 -
V V

A A
94. During the course of submissions, it was accepted by counsel for the
B Applicant that the question of whether a public reprimand alone was sufficient turned very B
much on this Tribunal’s view of the Applicant’s culpability. That must be correct.
C C
95. In the opinion of the Tribunal, that culpability must first be put into the
D D
context of the duties imposed on a sponsor in a listing application. Mention has been made
earlier in this determination of the importance of the sponsor’s role in listing applications.
E E
In this regard, in the Tribunal’s report in Sun Hung Kai International Limited, the following
F was said – F
G “It is clear to us that the regulatory framework insisting on the exercise of G
due diligence by each and every sponsor is critical to the orderly and
H transparent working of the market. That is why emphasis is placed on the H
dual obligation of a sponsor, an obligation not only to the client but, equally
importantly, to the integrity of the market.”
I I
96. The passage in Sun Hung Kai just quoted went on to state that investors
J J
must be able to assess the risk in buying shares in an initial public offering (and thereafter
K in day-to-day trading) by relying on accurate and relevant information contained in the K
listing documents. If they are unable to do that then trust in the market is undermined. In
L L
the present case, as stated earlier, if the regulators had not raised concern as to the possible
circular flow of funds, that concern may well have been reflected in the marketplace and
M M
may well have led to concerns as to the integrity not only of the of the listing but of the
N listing process itself. N
O
97. For this reason, in the opinion of the Tribunal, evidence of material
O
culpability on the part of a sponsor in the listing process will almost inevitably demand
P P
more than a public reprimand.
Q Q
(C) The fine
R R
98. While recognising that the Applicant had not been found culpable of any
S S
prior breach of regulatory conduct, the SFC nevertheless originally proposed a fine of
T HK$14 million. In its final decision, this was reduced to HK$4.5 million, the reduction, it T
appears, being made in light of the Applicant’s difficult financial circumstances.
U U
- 28 -
V V

A A
99. On behalf of the Applicant, it was submitted that this fine too was manifestly
B excessive. What had to be given more emphasis was the Applicant’s clear record, the fact B
that it had now ceased its sponsor business and, in particular, the dire circumstances of its
C C
finances at the relevant time, that is, in 2019.
D D
100. It was further said that, in light of the fees that had been earned13, if the
amount of the fine was not materially reduced, it would mean that the Applicant would
E E
suffer a significant loss in respect of the work it had done. In this regard, it was said that it
F had to be borne in mind that the findings of culpability related to one aspect only of the F
extensive due diligence work carried out by the Applicant. The Tribunal has difficulty
G G
accepting this submission. While all relevant circumstances must be taken into account,
including issues of financial jeopardy, it cannot be the case that a sponsor is entitled to
H H
make a profit in respect of work undertaken by it even though that work has been
I undermined by its own culpability. I
J
101. As to the quantum of the fine to be imposed in this case, the Tribunal
J
recognises that, while it is important to have regard to earlier cases in order to maintain
K K
some consistency of approach, it must be recognised that each case turns on its own
particular circumstances. It must also be recognised that, in respect of sanctions too, the
L L
Tribunal must approach the matter as if it is the original decision-maker.
M M
102. In the particular circumstances of this case, on a consideration of all the
N N
evidence, the Tribunal has concluded that the Applicant’s essential culpability lay in its
failure to look to the broader picture; to recognise that the dominant practice of third-party
O O
payments was, on its face at least, so unusual as to raise concern, a concern that was
P compounded when Aric Yip’s very substantial advance of funds to acquaintances for P
purposes of loans and/or investments was integrated into the overall picture. If sponsors
Q
are to fulfil their dual obligation to represent the interests of an applicant for listing and at
Q
the same time to protect the integrity of the market, the ability to adopt a fully objective
R R
view, that is, to step back and view matters as market participants may well view them, is
a necessary part of their professional skills.
S S
T 13 It was said that the total sponsor fee was HK$5 million, this fee being reduced by 50% because the listing T
ultimately lapsed, and what remained was significantly taken up with meeting salaries and administrative
expenses.
U U
- 29 -
V V

title: SFAT Application No. 4/2020 (Corrigendum)
text:
Application No. 4 of 2020
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures
Ordinance, Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
YI SHUN DA CAPITAL LIMITED Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Date of Hearing: 26 April 2021
Date of Determination: 19 October 2021

____________________
CORRIGENDUM
________________________
1. Page 13, paragraph 39, line J where “(paragraph 36)” should
read “(paragraph 37)”.
2. Page 16, footnote 9, line T where “top ten five customers”
should read “top ten/five customers”.
3. Page 18, paragraph 56(c), line A where “said that he had”
should read “said that she had”.
4. Page 28, paragraph 96, line M where “only of the of the”
should read “only of the”.
Dated 11th day of January 2022
Michael Hartmann
Chairman
Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal

title: SFAT Application No. 3/2019
text:
Application No. 3 of 2019
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF a Decision made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
sections 196 and 198 of the Securities and Futures
Ordinance, Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CAI HONGPING Applicant
and
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Date of Hearing: 5 & 6 October 2020
Date of Determination: 2 June 2021
____________________
DETERMINATION
_________________________

A A
The fraudulent listing of China Metal
B B
1. In June 2009, a company called China Metal Recycling (Holdings) Limited
C C
(“China Metal”) secured a listing on the Main Board of the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong
(“the Stock Exchange”). In terms of its prospectus, China Metal was in the business of
D D
dealing in, and recycling, scrap metals. Its main facilities were in Guangdong, Jiangsu and
Hong Kong. According to its prospectus, China Metal, acting through a wholly-owned
E E
subsidiary named Central Steel Macau, also dealt in scrap metal on the international market.
F F
2. Within months of the listing, concerns arose that, in its listing application,
G G
China Metal had deceived the market as to the true size of its business. This led to an
investigation into the affairs of the company by the Securities and Futures Commission
H H
(“the SFC”). The investigation revealed that there had been a dishonest inflation of the
I company’s business by means principally of the creation of fictitious transactions and I
circular flows of funds.
J J
3. In July 2013, the SFC presented a petition to the Court of First Instance to
K K
wind up China Metal. The petition was brought pursuant to s.212 of the Securities and
L Futures Ordinance, Cap 571 (“the Ordinance”) on the basis that it was desirable in the L
public interest. In ordering the winding up of the company1, the Court (per Harris J) made
M
the following observations:
M
N N
“In my view, the evidence adduced by the Commission [the SFC]
establishes that a fraud on a massive scale has been perpetrated by those in
O charge of the company on investors, the Stock Exchange and others O
involved in the listing of the company. It seems highly likely that Mr Chun
[the CEO of the company and a principal shareholder] caused the round
P P
robin transactions and the creation of bogus bills of lading with a view to
producing significantly better figures than would otherwise be the case. This
Q must have been done in order to advance the Initial Public Offering and Q
induce investors to subscribe for shares.”
R R
4. Earlier in his judgment, Harris J had cited figures showing that for the years
S 2007 to 2009 China Metal had, on one method of calculation, overstated its gross profit by S
a sum in excess of US$140 million, a 72% inflation. The judge found that this was as a
T T
1 See: Reasons for Decision dated 9 March 2015 (HCCW 210/2013).
U U
- 2 -
V V

A A
result of premeditated activity. As he said –
B B
“Clearly, this was a carefully planned and carefully implemented scheme to
C create accounts which significantly overstated the business and profit of the C
company for the purposes of the listing and thereafter. This was fraud on an
industrial scale which goes directly to the integrity of the listing. It is
D D
difficult to think of a clearer case of it being in the public interest that a
petition be brought by the Commission [the SFC] for a winding up.”
E E
The role of UBS in the listing of China Metal
F F
5. China Metal, in fact, made two applications for listing on the Main Board
G G
of the Stock Exchange, the first application being made in June 2008. On this first occasion,
UBS AG (“UBS”) acted as the sole sponsor, global coordinator, bookrunner and lead
H H
manager. The application, however, was referred back by the Stock Exchange on the basis
I that UBS at the time held certain debt securities issued by China Metal and that, if 15% of I
the net proceeds of the listing was applied to settle those debt securities, UBS would fall
J
into the category of a non-independent sponsor.
J
K K
6. When the second - successful - application for listing was made a year later
in June 2009, there were joint sponsors: UBS, as before, and China Merchants Securities
L L
(HK) Co. Ltd. (“CMS”). UBS, however, remained the global coordinator, bookrunner and
M lead manager. M
N N
Findings by the SFC into the failings of UBS as a sponsor
O O
7. When concerns arose as to the integrity of the China Metal’s listing, the
P SFC investigated the role of UBS as a sponsor, coming to the following determinations, P
namely, that –
Q Q
(a) UBS had failed to conduct adequate and reasonable due diligence enquiries
R R
in relation to China Metal’s listing and had failed to use all reasonable
efforts to ensure that the information and representations provided in the
S S
prospectus were true and accurate and were not misleading.
T T
U U
- 3 -
V V

A A
(b) That it had failed to keep a proper audit trail (that is, a written record) of the
B work done in respect of its due diligence investigations. B
C C
(c) That it had breached the sponsor’s undertaking made to the Stock Exchange
and/or had filed untrue statements in the sponsor's declaration.
D D
E E
(d) That it had failed to comply with all regulatory requirements applicable to
the conduct of a sponsor including the Listing Rules and, in particular,
F F
Practice Note 21 of those rules requiring the exercise of due diligence by
G sponsors in respect of listing applications. G
H H
8. In the result, on 14 March 2019 the SFC reprimanded UBS and imposed a
financial penalty upon it for its failure to discharge its duties as a sponsor and specifically
I I
for its breaches of the following –
J J
(a) General Principle 2 (related to the exercise of due diligence) of the Code of
K K
Conduct for persons licensed by and registered with the SFC (“the Code of
Conduct”) and paragraph 5.1 (related to the exercise of due skill and care)
L L
of the Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct (“the CFA Code of
M Conduct”). M
N N
(b) Paragraph 5.8 (related to the required standard of documentation) of the
CFA Code of Conduct.
O O
P P
(c) Paragraph 2.3 of the CFA Code of Conduct, together with certain guidelines,
related to the maintenance of proper books and records in order to ensure
Q Q
due compliance with this relevant legal and regulatory matters.
R R
(d) General principle 7 (related to compliance with regulatory requirements in
S S
order to promote the best interests and integrity of the market) of the Code
of Conduct and paragraph 1.5.1(3) of the Sponsor Guidelines.
T T
U U
- 4 -
V V

A A
9. UBS did not dispute the findings made by the SFC, namely, its culpability
B as sponsor, and accepted the sanctions imposed upon it. B
C C
10. UBS, of course, as a corporation, could only act through its Board, its
directors and its employees. Or, expressed in the round, through its management. Mr Cai
D D
Hongping (“the Applicant”) was at all material times a senior member of that management.
E E
Disciplinary action instituted against the Applicant personally
F F
11. By letter dated 26 September 2017, the SFC informed the Applicant that it
G G
had come to a preliminary view that the failings of UBS were to a material degree
attributable to negligence on his part in his capacity as the leader of the UBS transaction
H H
team (that is, in his capacity as the “Principal” or “Sponsor Principal”) and as a member of
I senior management of UBS. More particularly, in this regard, the Applicant was informed I
that a preliminary view had been reached that he had failed –
J J
(a) in breach of General Principle 2 to the Code of Conduct, to exercise due
K K
skill, care and diligence in the handling of China Metal’s listing application;
L L
(b) in breach of General Principle 9 of the Code of Conduct, to ensure the
M M
maintenance of appropriate standards of conduct and adherence to proper
procedures by UBS;
N N
O
(c) in breach of paragraph 4.2 of the Code of Conduct and paragraph 1.3.3 of
O
the Sponsors’ Guidelines, to diligently supervise subordinates and generally
P P
the sponsor work undertaken by UBS.
Q Q
12. In light of these asserted failings, the SFC informed the Applicant that it had
R R
come to the preliminary view that he was culpable of misconduct and/or was not a fit and
proper person to be licensed. Accordingly, pursuant to section 196 of the Ordinance2, the
S S
T 2 Section 196 of the Ordinance details the disciplinary action that may be exercised by the SFC when a T
regulated person has been found guilty of misconduct or when the SFC has reached a view that a regulated
person is not a fit and proper person to be or to remain regulated.
U U
- 5 -
V V

A A
SFC proposed to prohibit the Applicant from carrying on any regulated activities for a
B period of five years. B
C C
13. In January 2018, lengthy submissions in response were filed on behalf of
the Applicant. In those submissions, while it was accepted that the Applicant at the material
D D
time had been a member of senior management at UBS, it was denied that he had been the
leader - that is, the Principal - of the UBS transaction team tasked with navigating the China
E E
Metal listing. It followed that he should not be held personally liable for any lack of due
F diligence on the part of UBS. F
G G
14. On 3 October 2019, however, pursuant to a written ‘Decision Notice’ made
under sections 196 and 198 of the Ordinance, the SFC informed the Applicant that, having
H H
considered his response, it had nevertheless determined that he was personally culpable of
I misconduct and/or was not a fit and proper person to be licensed and that accordingly it I
had decided to prohibit him for a period of five years pursuant to section 196 of the
J
Ordinance from doing any of the following in respect of regulated activities –
J
K K
(a) from applying to be licensed or registered;
L L
(b) from applying to be approved pursuant to section 126(1) of the Ordinance
M M
as a ‘responsible officer’ of a licensed corporation;
N N
(c) from applying to be given consent to act or to continue to act as an
O
‘executive officer’ of a registered institution under section 71(C) of the
O
Banking Ordinance, and
P P
Q (d) from seeking through a registered institution to have his name entered in the Q
register maintained by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority under section 20
R R
of the Banking Ordnance as that of a person engaged by the registered
institution in respect of regulated activities.
S S
T T
U U
- 6 -
V V

A A
The Applicant’s grounds of review
B B
15. On 14 November 2019, the Applicant – pursuant to section 217 of the
C C
Ordinance - sought a review of the SFC's decision. In doing so, he put forward three
grounds of review which may be summarised as follows –
D D
(a) While the Applicant had been a senior member of UBS management, the
E E
SFC had fallen into error in finding that he had also been the ‘Principal’ or
F the ‘Sponsor Principal’, that is, the leader of the transaction team appointed F
by UBS for the China Metal listing. It was the Applicant’s assertion that he
G G
had never been appointed to head the transaction team. There was no direct,
unequivocal evidence of his appointment or indeed of any person’s
H H
appointment, to that position: no memorandum of appointment or anything
I similar. As for the indirect evidence, it was at best ambiguous and, viewed I
in the round, was insufficient to permit any adverse inference being drawn
J
against him.
J
K K
(b) That in any event the SFC had fallen into error in finding that the Applicant
had breached his duties under the Code of Conduct and/or the Sponsor
L L
Guidelines (or any other duties) bearing in mind the limited extent of his
M duties of supervision and his entirely reasonable reliance on the extensive M
due diligence efforts carried out by UBS and the Co-Sponsor, CMS, and, in
N N
particular, the Equity Commitment Committee of UBS (“the ECC”).
O O
(c) That, in respect of the sanctions imposed, they were manifestly excessive
and wholly disproportionate when considered in light of the role of the
P P
Applicant in the China Metal’s listing transaction and the nature of his
conduct in fulfilling that role.
Q Q
R R
The role of the Tribunal in this review
S S
16. The Tribunal is required to make a full merits review, conducting the review
T as if it is the original decision-maker. As to the standard of proof required to determine any T
question or issue before the Tribunal, section 218(7) of the Ordinance directs that it shall
U U
- 7 -
V V

A A
be the standard of proof applied in civil proceedings, that is, proof on a balance of
B probabilities. B
C C
17. As to the burden of proof, in light of the fact that the Tribunal’s role is to
conduct the review as if it is the original decision-maker, it follows that the SFC – as the
D D
party which has made findings and imposed sanctions - must still bear the burden of proof.
E E
18. In this review, with the agreement of the parties, the Chairman of the
F Tribunal sat alone.3 F
G G
The ‘fit and proper’ guidelines
H H
19. As set out above, one of the matters challenged by the Applicant is the
I determination made by the SFC that, by reason of his various acts of negligence while I
acting as the leader, that is, the Principal, of the UBS transaction team, he had shown
J
himself not to be a ‘fit and proper’ person pursuant to the relevant Guidelines to remain
J
licensed.
K K
20. The purpose of the ‘Fit and Proper Guidelines’ issued by the SFC is to
L L
ensure that persons applying for licences and registrations under the Ordinance are able to
M satisfy the SFC on a continuing basis that they are, and remain, fit and proper persons to M
be licensed or registered. As the guidelines express it:
N N

The nature (and importance) of ‘sponsorship’
B B
22. In Hong Kong, a corporation seeking a listing on the Stock Exchange is
C C
required to appoint at least one sponsor. A sponsor, often an investment bank, must hold a
licence to advise on matters of corporate finance4. A sponsor, essentially an expert
D D
corporate guide, is required to provide advice to the listing applicant to ensure that its
prospectus, and any accompanying materials, have met the requirements of all relevant
E E
regulations and rules. A sponsor must undertake all necessary due diligence to seek to
F ensure the accuracy and adequacy of the materials upon which a listing application is F
founded to enable potential investors to come to an informed opinion of the financial
G G
condition of the applicant seeking to list. To discharge this obligation, a sponsor must not
only work closely with an applicant, challenging that applicant when legitimate questions
H H
arise, but it must use all reasonable endeavours to address matters raised by the Stock
I Exchange in connection with the application. I
J
23. The importance of the exercise of due diligence by corporate financial
J
advisers acting as sponsors has been emphasised by the SFC on a number of occasions,
K K
particularly in a document entitled Consultation Conclusions on the Regulation of Sponsors
and Independent Financial Advisers published in October 2004:
L L
M “Due diligence by sponsors is important because… the Exchange (‘the M
Stock Exchange’) places significant reliance on this work. The Exchange
does not have the resources or mandate to gain the detailed knowledge of
N N
an issuer’s business which the sponsor is expected to have accumulated
through its preparation of the applicant for listing. Consequently, the
O Exchange and the market are entitled to rely on the competence and integrity O
of the sponsor in assisting the issuer to prepare and present the listing
application and listing documents.”
P P
24. In an earlier determination in 20145, this Tribunal confirmed the central
Q Q
importance of the role of a sponsor –
R R
“It is clear to us that the regulatory framework insisting on the exercise of
due diligence by each and every sponsor is critical to the orderly and
S S
4 A Type 6 regulated activity.
T T
5 Sun Hung Kai International Limited and the Securities and Futures Commission, judgment dated 27
January 2014, paragraph 44.
U U
- 9 -
V V

A A
transparent working of the market. That is why emphasis is placed on the
dual obligation of a sponsor, an obligation not only to the client but, equally
B B
importantly, to the integrity of the market. In an orderly and transparent
market, investors must be able to place trust, first, in the fact that the listing
C of a company has been founded by the Stock Exchange on the consideration C
of full and accurate information and, second, that the information contained
in the prospectus is also full and accurate. The route to ensuring such trust
D D
must rest principally on the sponsor, the party responsible for the
management of the listing. It must rest principally on the conduct of an
objective, professional and scrupulous investigation of all material relevant
E E
to the listing and the initial public offering; in short on the conduct of
reasonable due diligence. All investment involves risk. The point is that
F investors must be able to assess the risk by relying on accurate and relevant F
information. If they are unable to do that then trust in the market is
undermined.”

104. Determining the factual issue of whether the Applicant, to his own
Q Q

balance of probabilities has demonstrated that the Applicant was, to his own knowledge,
D D
the appointed leader.
